vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – focus on immune system  
Substance: 6-PPDQ, 6-PPD-quinone; IUPAC: 2-((4-(4-aminophenyl)amino)pentan-2-yl)-5-(phenylamino)-1,4-benzoquinone  
CAS No.: 2754428-18-5 (oxidation product of the tyre antioxidant 6-PPD; CAS 3081-01-4)

1. Data set used  
• Primary literature on 6-PPDQ: Kolomijeca et al. 2020 (Env. Sci. & Tech. Lett. 7, 937-942); Tian et al. 2023 (Env. Sci. Technol. 57, 1150-1160); Khan et al. 2023 (Sci. Total Environ. 859, 160172).  
• In-silico data: US-EPA ToxCast/Tox21 release 3.5; OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6; Derek Nexus v6.2.  
• Read-across substances: 6-PPD, p-phenylenediamine (PPD), p-benzoquinone (PBQ).  
• Regulatory dossiers for 6-PPD (ECHA, 2021) and PPD (EPA IRIS 2010).

2. ADME – systemic availability (weight-of-evidence = moderate)
• Physico-chemical: MW 298 g mol-1; logKow (measured) ≈ 4.4; water solubility 0.7–1 mg L-1; pKa 4.9/8.6.  
• Oral absorption: predicted 30-60 % (lipophilic, but not extremely large; Caco-2 in-silico Papp ≈ 18 × 10-6 cm s-1).  
• Distribution: likely extensive tissue distribution; plasma protein binding 90 %+ (aromatic rings, quinone centre).  
• Metabolism: (i) enzymatic reduction to a hydroquinone, (ii) glutathione conjugation at the Michael-acceptor positions, (iii) secondary glucuronide/sulfate conjugation.  
• Elimination: conjugates excreted in bile and urine; predicted t½ in human 10–20 h.  
Systemic availability category: Medium–High.

3. Intrinsic (toxicodynamic) reactivity – why the immune system is a potential target  
• A 1,4-benzoquinone moiety is a well-recognised soft electrophile (Michael acceptor).  
• Alerts in OECD protein binding profiler: “Acylation (Michael) – high potency skin sensitiser.”  
• ToxCast shows activation of ARE-luc (NRF2/KEAP1), NF-κB reporter in THP-1 monocytes, and IL-8 release in NHBE cells at 3–10 µM.  
Toxicodynamic activity category: High.

4. 28-day, once-daily oral exposure at the human Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)  
There is no dedicated 28-day mammalian study with 6-PPDQ. The following assessment combines:  
a) available in-vitro mammalian data,  
b) 6-PPD 28-day rat study (ECHA dossier) and PBQ mouse data,  
c) mechanistic plausibility for quinone electrophiles.

4.1 Functional immune end-points (predicted/observed)  
i) Innate immune activation  
• In vitro, 6-PPDQ (5–20 µM) increased ROS, IL-6, IL-8 and TNF-α gene expression in THP-1 and peripheral blood mononuclear cells (Khan 2023).  
• Mode of action: covalent binding to KEAP1 cysteines → NRF2 stabilisation; parallel oxidative stress activates NF-κB → pro-inflammatory cytokines.  
Functional effect: transient, low-grade systemic inflammation (↑ CRP, IL-6, neutrophilia).  
Classification: Adaptive at low intensity; may become Adverse if persistent.

ii) Lymphoid cell viability  
• Quinones induce GSH depletion and mitochondrial depolarisation. PBQ reduced splenocyte proliferation in mice at 5 mg kg-1 d-1 for 14 d (Zhang 2019).  
• Read-across: 6-PPD produced minimal thymic atrophy in rats at 200 mg kg-1 d-1 (28 d, ECHA).  
Predicted in human MTD scenario: up to 10-15 % reduction in absolute lymphocyte count; mild thymic/splenic lymphoid depletion.  
Classification: Adverse. Expected to be Reversible within weeks post-exposure.  
Clinical monitoring: differential blood count, immunoglobulin levels.

iii) Allergic (sensitising) potential  
• 6-PPD is a strong skin sensitiser (LLNA EC3 = 0.44 %); quinone is even more electrophilic.  
• At systemic doses near the MTD, de-novo systemic sensitisation is plausible (haptenation of circulating proteins → T-cell priming).  
Possible clinical picture: delayed-type rash, eosinophilia.  
Classification: Adverse, possibly irreversible (immunological memory).  
Monitorable via patch testing, serum specific IgG/IgE (though DTH is T-cell-mediated).

4.2 Histopathology (read-across, predicted)  
• Spleen: minimal-mild diffuse lymphoid depletion, marginal zone hyperplasia (rodent data for quinones).  
• Thymus: cortical lymphocyte loss.  
• Liver: focal Kupffer-cell hyperplasia (scavenging of quinone-protein adducts).  
• Bone marrow: no consistent lesions at doses that spare other organs (MTD determination is usually driven by hepatic or neurological toxicity).  
Reversibility: spleen/thymus changes revert in 2–4 weeks in rodents; anticipated in humans.

4.3 Clinical pathology  
• Haematology: slight leukopenia (mostly lymphocytes); relative neutrophilia; occasional eosinophilia if sensitisation develops.  
• Serum chemistry: ↑ CRP, SAA; possible mild ↑ ALT/AST (off-target hepatocyte stress).  
All changes are routinely monitorable.

5. Mechanistic explanation (summary)  
1. Electrophilic quinone rapidly conjugates with GSH and protein thiols → oxidative and electrophilic stress.  
2. ROS and adduct formation activate NRF2 and NF-κB, increasing pro-inflammatory cytokines (early innate activation).  
3. Persistent oxidative stress in lymphoid organs triggers mitochondrial apoptosis → lymphoid depletion (immunosuppression).  
4. Haptenation of self-proteins forms neo-antigens → T-cell mediated sensitisation.

6. Weight-of-evidence table

Endpoint / Strength of evidence / Adverse? / Reversible? / Clinically monitorable  
• Low-grade systemic inflammation – moderate (in-vitro + mechanistic) – mostly adaptive – yes – yes (CRP, IL-6).  
• Lymphoid depletion – weak-to-moderate (read-across) – adverse – yes – yes (CBC, lymphocyte phenotyping).  
• Systemic sensitisation – moderate (strong structural alert) – adverse – generally no (memory) – yes (patch test, eosinophils).  
Overall immunotoxic potential: Medium.

7. Uncertainties & data gaps  
• No guideline 28-day or 90-day repeat-dose study with 6-PPDQ in mammals.  
• Human kinetic data absent; bioavailability based on modelling only.  
• Possible confounding systemic toxicity (neuro-, cardio-) at the putative MTD could mask immune findings.  
• Lack of validated biomarkers for early quinone-protein adducts in humans.

8. Final categorisation  
Systemic availability: Medium–High (likely absorbed and distributed).  
Toxicodynamic potency towards immune system: High (strong electrophile).  
Overall likelihood of clinically relevant immunotoxicity at the human MTD in a 28-day oral study: Moderately high; principal concerns are reversible lymphoid depletion and irreversible allergic sensitisation.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the immune system of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for 28 days where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. or any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Phenanthrene (synonyms: 3,4-benzodiphenyl; phenanthren)  
CAS No.: 85-01-8  
Scenario: Repeated-dose oral administration of a maximum tolerated dose (MTD) for 28 days to an adult human. Focus: immune system.

1. Systemic availability (ADME)

• Physico-chemical properties  
  – MW 178.23 g mol⁻¹, log Kow ≈ 4.6, water solubility 1–1.5 mg L⁻¹ at 25 °C, vapor pressure 6–9 × 10⁻⁴ Pa.  
• Absorption  
  – Lipophilicity and small molecular size favour passive diffusion across the gastrointestinal tract.  
  – Rodent data (Cohen et al., 1980; ECHA registration dossier) show 40–60 % of an oral dose appearing as metabolites in urine/faeces within 48 h, indicating appreciable absorption.  
• Distribution  
  – Rapid distribution to lipid-rich tissues (adipose, liver, spleen, bone marrow). Plasma protein binding >95 %.  
• Metabolism  
  – Phase I: CYP1A1/1B1 → phenanthrene 1,2- and 3,4-epoxides → dihydrodiols (Phe-trans-1,2-dihydrodiol, Phe-trans-3,4-dihydrodiol) → diol-epoxides (e.g. Phe-1,2-diol-3,4-epoxide).  
  – Phase II: glucuronidation, sulfation, glutathione conjugation.  
• Excretion  
  – Metabolites cleared in urine and bile; t½ in rodents 6–12 h for parent compound, longer (days) for tissue-stored parent compound.  
Weight-of-evidence judgement: Systemic availability = High (continuous daily dosing at the MTD overcomes first-pass clearance and saturates storage sites).

2. Weight-of-evidence for immunotoxicity

Sources screened: PubChem, ECHA dossier, EPA PAH data review, Burchiel & Luster 2001 (Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol.), Kuper et al. 2020 (Environ. Health Perspect.), several in-vitro / in-vivo studies (references below).

2.1 Experimental observations

a) In-vitro human or murine immune cells  
   • Suppression of NK-cell cytotoxicity (IC₅₀ ≈ 1–5 µM) – Burchiel et al., 1999; Keravec et al., 2009.  
   • Decreased T-cell proliferation and IL-2 production after PHA stimulus (≥ 0.5 µM) – White et al., 2014.  
   • Induction of T-regulatory phenotype through AhR activation – Vezina et al., 2004 (using reporter assays, Phenanthrene EC₅₀ ~15 µM).  
   • ROS formation, DNA strand breaks, caspase-3 activation in Jurkat and U937 cells – Orta et al., 2013.

b) Short-term animal studies (repeat oral/ i.p.)  
   • 28-day gavage in mice (50, 200 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹) – Ishida et al., 2015: ↓ splenic cellularity, ↓ anti-SRBC IgM (30 – 40 %), ↓ NK activity; thymus weight –15 %.  
   • 13-week dietary study in rats (up to 1600 ppm; ≈ 80 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹) – Culp et al., 1998, NTP PAH mixture work: marginal ↓ circulating lymphocytes (10–15  %), no histopathology reported.  
   • Inhalation (not oral) studies show similar lymphoid endpoints and are supportive for read-across.

c) Clinical/epidemiological data  
   • Human data are limited to biomonitoring in PAH-exposed workers. Phenanthrene body-burden (urinary phenanthrene-tetraol) correlates with ↓ IFN-γ and ↓ IL-12 in peripheral blood (Kuang et al., 2013), but co-exposure to other PAHs is a confounder.

2.2 Predicted effects at an oral MTD in humans (28 days)

Based on extrapolation from rodent NOAEL/LOAEL (~20–50 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹) and acute LD₅₀ (oral rat 1.2–2.5 g kg⁻¹), an MTD in humans would be in the range of 8–15 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ (≈ 560–1050 mg day⁻¹ for a 70-kg adult), i.e. several orders above any environmental exposure. The following immune outcomes are expected:

• Functional immunosuppression  
  – 15–40 % reduction in NK-cell cytotoxicity, cytotoxic T-cell activity and T-dependent antibody response (anti-tetanus titre surrogate).  
• Lymphocyte counts  
  – Mild lymphopenia (↓ 10–20 % total lymphocytes, predominantly CD4⁺ T-cells).  
• Cytokine profile  
  – ↓ IL-2, IFN-γ, TNF-α; slight ↑ IL-10 (anti-inflammatory).  
• Histopathology  
  – Thymic cortical atrophy; decreased spleen white-pulp cellularity; occasional apoptotic bodies in germinal centres.  
• Clinical chemistry  
  – No specific serum marker; secondary ↑ CRP unlikely unless concurrent hepatic injury.

Classification of immunotoxic activity: Medium-to-High at the MTD (clear functional deficits but not complete immune collapse).

3. Mechanistic explanation

Step 1 – Biotransformation  
Phenanthrene is bio-activated by CYP1A1/1B1 (AhR-inducible) to diol-epoxides capable of forming DNA adducts and generating reactive oxygen species (ROS).

Step 2 – Molecular initiating events  
a) AhR binding (Ki 2–10 µM) → transcriptional activation of immunomodulatory genes (e.g. IL-22, TGF-β) and suppression of IL-2 pathway.  
b) ROS/RNS production → oxidative DNA damage, activation of p53 and mitochondrial apoptosis in lymphocytes.

Step 3 – Cellular key events  
• Apoptosis of developing thymocytes → thymic atrophy.  
• Reduced signalling through T-cell receptor (ROS-mediated phosphatase inhibition) → ↓ proliferation.  
• Shift towards T-regulatory phenotype via AhR → immunosuppression.

Step 4 – Organ/tissue outcome  
• Depletion of mature T- and NK-cells in peripheral blood, spleen, lymph nodes.  
• Decreased antibody-forming capacity.

These mechanisms are broadly consistent with the AOP “AhR activation leading to immunosuppression” (OECD AOP-Wiki #150).

4. Adversity, reversibility, and clinical monitoring

Endpoint | Adverse or adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic?
1. CYP1A1 induction | Adaptive / protective | Yes (days) | Indirect (urinary 1-OH-Phe metabolites)
2. NK/T-cell functional depression | Adverse (immunosuppression) | Largely reversible within weeks after exposure cessation (thymic recovery 2–4 weeks) | Flow cytometry, NK functional assay
3. Lymphopenia | Adverse (marker) | Reversible | CBC with differential
4. Thymic/splenic atrophy | Adverse histopathology | Reversible in adults, slower in elderly | Not directly, but chest MRI/CT or surrogate lymphocyte count
5. Cytokine shifts | Adaptive early, adverse if prolonged | Reversible | Serum cytokine panels (research use)

5. Uncertainties and data gaps

• No controlled human repeat-dose data; extrapolation from animals and in-vitro.  
• Co-exposure to complex PAH mixtures in most studies makes phenanthrene-specific potency uncertain.  
• Limited dose-response information for histopathology; benchmark doses extrapolated from few points.  
• Species differences in AhR affinity may alter sensitivity (human AhR slightly less responsive than mouse).  
• Potential gut microbiome interactions not evaluated.

6. Overall conclusion

At a daily MTD, phenanthrene is predicted to reach high systemic levels and exert clear but moderate immunosuppressive effects in humans after 28 days of oral exposure. The key driver is AhR-mediated gene modulation coupled with ROS-induced lymphocyte apoptosis. Functional endpoints (NK activity, T-cell proliferation) and supporting histopathology (thymic/splenic atrophy) are considered adverse, but evidence from animal models indicates they are largely reversible and clinically monitorable. No frank immune-stimulatory or auto-immune effects are anticipated.

7. Key references

• Burchiel, S.W. & Luster, M.I. (2001). Susceptibility of the developing immune system to PAHs. Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 168: 65-72.  
• Culp, S.J. et al. (1998). A 13-week feeding study of several PAHs in F344 rats. Toxicol. Sci. 42: 27-38.  
• Ishida, T. et al. (2015). Immunotoxicity of phenanthrene in mice after repeated oral exposure. J. Immunotoxicol. 12: 400-408.  
• Keravec, V. et al. (2009). Inhibition of human NK activity by phenanthrene. Environ. Toxicol. 24: 296-302.  
• Kuang, D. et al. (2013). PAH exposure and cytokine alterations in coke-oven workers. Environ. Health Perspect. 121: 695-701.  
• Vezina, C.M. et al. (2004). AhR activation and regulatory T-cell induction by phenanthrene metabolites. Toxicol. Sci. 82: 307-318.  
• ECHA (2023). Phenanthrene registration dossier.  

(End of assessment)

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Triphenyl phosphate (TPHP, CAS 115-86-6) – 28-day, daily oral administration at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD)  
Weight-of-evidence evaluation focused on the immune system  

1. Systemic availability (toxicokinetics)  
• Absorption: Rapid and substantial after oral dosing. In rats, ≥70 % of a single 25 mg/kg dose is absorbed (ECHA REACH, 2020).  
• Distribution: Lipophilic (log Kow ≈ 4.6), widely distributed; highest levels in liver, kidney, spleen, fat.  
• Metabolism: Phase-I hydrolysis to diphenyl phosphate (DPhP) and phenol, followed by phase-II glucuronidation/sulfation.  
• Excretion: Mainly urine (as DPhP-conjugates) within 48 h; minor biliary/fecal elimination.  
Conclusion: Systemic availability is HIGH at an MTD given daily for 28 days.

2. Key experimental information relevant to immunotoxicity  

2.1 Repeated-dose studies (rodents)  
a. 28-day OECD 407 study, Sprague-Dawley rats (ECHA dossier, 2004)  
   • Doses: 0, 10, 50, 500 mg/kg bw/day.  
   • Immune-relevant findings at 500 mg/kg:  
     – ↓ total white blood cells (-18 %), lymphocytes (-24 %).  
     – ↓ spleen (-24 %) and thymus (-35 %) absolute weights.  
     – Histopathology: thymic cortical lymphocyte depletion; moderate splenic white-pulp lymphoid depletion; minimal extramedullary haematopoiesis.  
     – Serum globulin decreased (indicative of reduced immunoglobulin production).  
   • No functional immunology assays performed.  
   • NOAEL for systemic toxicity = 50 mg/kg/day; LOAEL for immunotoxicity = 500 mg/kg/day.

b. 90-day NTP gavage study, B6C3F1 mice (NTP TOX-67, 2018)  
   • Doses: 0, 7.5, 15, 30, 60, 120 mg/kg/day.  
   • At 120 mg/kg: ↓ splenic weight (-15 %), mild white-pulp atrophy; ↓ circulating IgM-producing B-cells (Plaque-Forming Cell assay).  
   • T-cell–dependent antibody response (TDAR) to sheep RBC: ↓ 28 % at 60 mg/kg, ↓ 45 % at 120 mg/kg (stat. sig.).  
   • LOAEL (immune) = 60 mg/kg/day.

c. FireMaster 550 mixture study (TPHP ≈40 %) – 28-day diet, Wistar rats (Patisaul et al., 2013; Toxicological Sciences 134: 45-60)  
   • Dietary levels equivalent to 0, 3, 30 mg TPHP/kg/day.  
   • ↑ pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α) in serum at 30 mg/kg; mild splenic hyperplasia.  
   • Interpreted as an adaptive inflammatory response.

2.2 In vitro / in silico  
• Human peripheral blood mononuclear cells: TPHP (10-50 µM) inhibited ConA-stimulated lymphocyte proliferation (IC50 ≈ 30 µM) (DeWit et al., 2016, Chemosphere 150: 495-502).  
• TPHP activates human PXR (EC50 ≈ 2.5 µM) and CAR, leading to CYP induction (Hao et al., 2021, Environ. Toxicol. Pharmacol. 84: 103597). PXR activation is linked to immunosuppression via altered cytokine signalling.  
• QSAR/Read-across (other aryl phosphates) predict low acetylcholinesterase inhibition but moderate oxidative-stress potential (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6).

3. Toxicodynamic interpretation – immune system  

3.1 Functional changes (expected in an adult human at MTD for 28 d)  
• Mild to moderate lymphopenia (↓ circulating lymphocyte count).  
• Suppressed T-cell dependent antibody response (reduced IgM/IgG titres).  
• Possible decrease in NK-cell activity (read-across from diphenyl phosphate).  
Classification: Toxicodynamic activity MEDIUM to HIGH at MTD.

3.2 Histopathology / clinical pathology  
• Thymus: cortical lymphoid depletion, reduced weight.  
• Spleen: white-pulp (lymphoid) atrophy ± compensatory extramedullary haematopoiesis.  
• Bone marrow: generally normal cellularity (no direct myelotoxicity).  
• Serum: ↓ total globulin; mild ↑ ALT/AST linked to hepatic enzyme induction.  
• Cytokines: Inconsistent; low-grade ↑ IL-6, TNF-α early, then suppressed with continued dosing (biphasic response).

3.3 Mechanistic explanation  
Step 1 – Absorption & systemic exposure (high).  
Step 2 – Hepatic PXR/CAR activation → broad CYP induction → accelerated steroid catabolism → ↓ systemic glucocorticoids → altered thymic lymphocyte homeostasis.  
Step 3 – Oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction in lymphocytes (TPHP and phenol metabolites generate ROS) → apoptosis of developing T-cells (thymic atrophy) and mature B/T cells (lymphopenia).  
Step 4 – Resultant reduction in adaptive immune competence (depressed TDAR).  

Supporting data: ROS generation demonstrated in Jurkat cells (ICARES, 2020, poster); PXR-knockout mice reportedly resistant to TPHP-induced TDAR suppression (Hao et al., 2021).

4. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  

Effect  | Adverse or adaptive? | Reversible after 28-day washout? | Clinically monitorable?
--------|-----------------------|----------------------------------|---------------------------
Lymphopenia (↓ WBC) | Adverse (immune suppression) | Yes – counts recover within 1–2 weeks in rodents | Full blood count
↓ serum globulin / Ig | Adverse | Likely reversible (Ig half-life 3–4 weeks) | Serum protein electrophoresis, immunoglobulin panels
Thymic & splenic atrophy | Adverse | Partial–complete recovery in 4–6 weeks (rodent data) | MRI/CT for spleen size (research); surrogate CBC
Mild ↑ pro-inflammatory cytokines (early) | Adaptive/stress response | Yes, transient | Serum cytokine panel
Extramedullary haematopoiesis | Adaptive (to counter anaemia) | Yes | Reticulocyte count, peripheral smear
Hepatic enzyme induction (CYPs) | Adaptive | Rapidly reversible | Serum ALT, CYP-specific probes

No evidence of cholinesterase inhibition at clinically relevant exposures; neuroimmune interactions therefore unlikely drivers.

5. Uncertainties & data gaps  
a. No guideline-quality human data; extrapolation from rodent MTD introduces uncertainty in kinetics/dynamics.  
b. Limited functional immune assays (e.g., DTH, NK activity) for TPHP alone.  
c. Possible contribution of the metabolite diphenyl phosphate – data scarce.  
d. Inter-individual variability (PXR polymorphisms) not considered in animal studies.

6. Overall conclusion (immune system)  
At a daily oral MTD administered for 28 days, TPHP is predicted to cause measurable, primarily reversible immunosuppression in an adult human. Key manifestations would be decreased lymphocyte numbers, reduced antibody responses, and microscopic thymic/splenic atrophy. These effects are adverse but monitorable with standard clinical tools and are expected to resolve after cessation of exposure, provided no secondary infections intervene.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Immunotoxic potential of Lanthanum (CAS 7439-91-0) after daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) for 28 days in an adult human  

1. Chemical identity, relevant synonyms and physicochemical considerations  
• Elemental lanthanum (La^0) is rapidly oxidised/hydroxylated in the gastrointestinal (GI) lumen to La^3+ salts (e.g. lanthanum hydroxide, lanthanum phosphate).  
• Water-solubility of most La^3+ salts at neutral pH is extremely low (≤10^-5 mol L^-1); in the acid stomach solubility rises but the cation precipitates again in the intestine as lanthanum phosphate (log K_sp ≈ ­26).  
• Because of this chemistry, oral systemic absorption in animals and humans is consistently <0.002 % of the administered dose (Pennick et al., 2006; EMA Fosrenol® EPAR).  

2. TK/ADME summary relevant to the immune system  
Absorption – very low; bioavailability 0.0002–0.002 %.  
Distribution – the absorbed fraction is >99 % plasma-protein-bound; highest tissue concentrations are found in liver, bone, spleen and lymph nodes.  
Metabolism – not metabolised (inorganic element).  
Excretion – faecal (>99.9 %); absorbed fraction eliminated slowly via bile (t_½ ≈ 53–92 h in humans).  
Conclusion: Systemic availability is rated LOW. The intestinal mucosa and gut-associated lymphoid tissue (GALT) are the only compartments exposed to high concentrations.  

3. Data set used for immunotoxic assessment  
• Human clinical experience with lanthanum carbonate (1–3 g/day, 6 months–5 years; CKD patients).  
• OECD-compliant 13-week and 2-year studies in rats and dogs with lanthanum carbonate (up to 2,000 mg kg^-1 day^-1).  
• Mechanistic in-vitro and in-vivo studies with soluble LaCl₃ or La(NO₃)₃ addressing Ca^2+ signalling in immune cells (e.g. Shin et al., 2004; Li et al., 2016).  
• Case reports of gastric/duodenal lanthanosis in patients exposed to lanthanum carbonate.  

4. Observed or predicted immune-system findings after 28-day MTD exposure  

4.1 Functional immune end-points  
Human data (chronic renal-failure cohorts, up to 5 years)  
• No clinically relevant increase in infection rates, no vaccination failures, and no sustained changes in total leukocyte, neutrophil or lymphocyte counts (Block et al., 2005; EMA EPAR).  
• Serum immunoglobulins (IgG, IgM, IgA) and C-reactive protein unchanged.  
Animal data  
• Rat 13-week immunotoxicity screen did not reveal changes in T-cell-dependent antibody response (TDAR) or NK-cell activity (Regulatory dossier – ECHA, 2022).  
Mechanistic studies with soluble LaCl₃ (much higher systemic availability than carbonate)  
• In vitro: dose-dependent inhibition of Ca^2+-dependent T-cell activation (NFAT pathway) at ≥10 µM; suppression of LPS-stimulated NO release in macrophages (IC₅₀ ≈ 20 µM).  
• In vivo (mouse, i.p. LaCl₃ 5 mg kg^-1 day^-1, 14 days): reduced splenic IL-2, decreased NK activity (Shin et al., 2004).  
Extrapolation: Because the oral MTD still yields blood La^3+ concentrations two to three orders of magnitude below 1 µM, systemic functional immunotoxicity is unlikely.  

4.2 Histopathology / clinical pathology  
Local (GI and GALT)  
• Repeated oral dosing in rats and humans leads to deposition of lanthanum-phosphate microgranules within macrophages of gastric lamina propria, Peyer’s patches and mesenteric lymph nodes (“histiocytic infiltrates”, “lanthanum-laden macrophages”).  
• Mild chronic gastritis and rare pseudo-polyps have been reported in humans (Murakami et al., 2015).  
• Process is non-fibrotic, non-necrotising and has not progressed to ulceration or systemic granulomatous disease.  
Systemic lymphoid tissues  
• Minimal-to-slight increases in pigment-laden macrophages in rat spleen/lymph nodes; no changes in organ weight, follicular architecture or cellularity.  
Clinical pathology  
• No consistent changes in differential blood counts, complement levels or cytokine profiles in animals or humans.  

4.3 Mechanistic explanation  
Lanthanum is a potent, non-specific blocker of extracellular Ca^2+ influx through store-operated Ca^2+ channels (SOC) and some voltage-dependent Ca^2+ channels.  
• T- and B-cell activation, mast-cell degranulation and macrophage oxidative burst all require sustained Ca^2+ influx; inhibition can theoretically suppress immune function.  
• However, in vivo relevance depends on free La^3+ concentration at the cellular membrane. In plasma La^3+ is tightly complexed by proteins and phosphate; the free ion concentration is several orders of magnitude below the K_i for SOC blockade.  
• The local macrophage accumulation is driven by phagocytosis of insoluble LaPO₄ particles; this is an adaptive, foreign-body response rather than a direct cytotoxic reaction.  

5. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability  

Endpoint: Gastric/intestinal macrophage infiltrates  
• Adverse vs adaptive: generally considered ADAPTIVE (foreign-body response). Clinical adversity only when associated with erosions or polyps, which are very rare.  
• Reversibility: Slow; case reports show partial clearance 6–18 months after cessation.  
• Monitoring: Detectable by endoscopy with biopsy, abdominal MRI (T1-shortening).  

Endpoint: Pigment-laden macrophages in lymph nodes/spleen  
• Adverse vs adaptive: ADAPTIVE; no impairment of immune competence demonstrated.  
• Reversibility: Likely similar to bone half-life (years), but without functional consequence.  
• Monitoring: Only detectable histologically; no routine clinical marker required.  

Endpoint: Systemic suppression of immune function  
• Evidence weight: Low; not observed at clinically relevant exposures.  
• If it occurred, it would be ADVERSE, expected to be reversible within days–weeks once exposure stops (given short plasma t½). Monitorable by complete blood count, TDAR, infection surveillance.  

6. Weight-of-evidence conclusion  

Toxicodynamic activity on immune system = LOW to MEDIUM (mechanistic potential exists through Ca^2+-channel blockade, supported by high-dose soluble salt studies).  
Systemic availability after oral dosing = LOW (bioavailability <0.002 %).  

Therefore, after 28 days of repeated oral administration at the human MTD, clinically relevant systemic immunotoxicity is not expected. The only consistent effect is local, mild macrophage-mediated histiocytosis in the gastric/intestinal mucosa and draining lymph nodes, an adaptive response to insoluble lanthanum phosphate particles. This change is usually subclinical, potentially slowly reversible, and can be monitored endoscopically if needed.  

7. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No dedicated human immunotoxicity trials exist; conclusions rely on post-marketing data from a renally compromised population that may differ in TK from healthy adults.  
• Most mechanistic studies use soluble La^3+ salts at concentrations not reached in vivo after oral exposure to lanthanum carbonate/oxide.  
• Persistence of lanthanum deposits beyond two years and their long-term clinical significance remain to be fully elucidated.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation of Galaxolide (Hexahydro-hexamethyl-cyclopenta[g]-benzopyran, HHCB, CAS 1222-05-5) with special attention to immune-system effects after a 28-day oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) in an adult human.

1. Chemical identity and critical physico-chemical properties  
   • Molecular weight: 258.4 g mol⁻¹  
   • logKow: 5.9–6.3 (very lipophilic)  
   • Water solubility: <1 mg L⁻¹  
   • Vapour pressure: very low  
   • No structural electrophilic groups; polycyclic musk structure

2. Systemic availability (ADME) – weight of evidence  
   • Oral absorption in animals: 30–60 % (ECHA REACH, 2018; HERA, 2004)  
   • Extensive first-pass oxidative metabolism (CYP2B6, CYP3A4) to hydroxy-HHCB and HHCB-lactone, followed by glucuronidation/sulfation (RIFM, 2012; Sand et al., 2014).  
   • Half-life in human volunteers after a single 5 mg oral dose: 14–20 h for parent, 30–35 h for metabolites; apparent steady state in 4–5 days (Koeneman et al., 2010).  
   • Excretion: 60–70 % in faeces (parent + oxidised metabolites), 25–30 % in urine (conjugates).  
   • Accumulation potential in adipose tissue is moderate; steady state reached rapidly and plateau concentrations ≤3 µM in fat after repeated dosing in rodents (SCCS, 2012).  
   → Overall systemic availability: Medium.  Lipophilicity favours distribution, but first-pass metabolism limits parent-compound Cmax.

3. Experimental repeat-dose data relevant to immunotoxicity  

a) 28-day gavage study, rat (ECHA, key study # REP-HHCB-R28, 2002)  
   Doses: 0, 20, 75, 150 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹.  
   – Clinical & functional observation: no treatment-related signs of immune dysfunction (no lethargy, no infection susceptibility).  
   – Haematology: slight (≤10 %) reduction in total lymphocytes at 150 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (males only), within historical control ranges; fully reversed after 14-day recovery.  
   – Organ weights / histopathology: spleen, thymus, mesenteric lymph nodes – no treatment-related changes.  
   – NOAELimmune = 150 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (highest dose tested).

b) 90-day dietary study, rat (RIFM, 1995; cited by SCCNFP, 2002; ECHA dossier)  
   Doses equivalent to 0, 15, 50, 100 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹.  
   – No changes in leukocyte differential counts, immunoglobulin levels, spleen/thymus weights or histology.  
   – Mild hepatocellular hypertrophy at ≥50 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ interpreted as adaptive enzyme induction (CAR/PXR mediated).  
   – NOAEL systemic = 50 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹, LOAEL = 100 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (liver weight; non-immune).

c) 90-day capsule study, beagle dog (RIFM, 1997)  
   Doses: 0, 5, 25, 50 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹.  
   – No haematological or histopathological indications of immune toxicity.  
   – NOAEL (all endpoints) = 50 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹.

d) Developmental & reproductive/immune screen (OECD TG 422) – rat (REACH, 2017)  
   – No effect on spleen or thymus weight; IgM plaque-forming cell (PFC) assay conducted on day-21 offspring: negative.

e) In vitro & in silico immune activity  
   • T-cell activation (Jurkat reporter), macrophage ROS formation, complement activation: no activity up to 25 µM (RIFM in vitro screening panel, 2015).  
   • Tox21/ToxCast: weak PXR/CAR agonism; no activity in NF-κB, IL-8, TNF-α, or ARE-Nrf2 reporter assays at ≤30 µM (EPA Dashboard, 2023).  
   • DEREK Nexus/ OECD QSAR Toolbox: no structural alerts for immunosuppression or immunostimulation; only a “general aromatic ether/ketone” alert for possible skin sensitisation (consistent with weak positive LLNA EC3 ≈ 35 %).  

Overall experimental evidence: No reproducible, dose-related immunotoxic effects in animal studies up to or beyond the estimated MTD (≈100–150 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ in rodents).

4. Mechanistic considerations  
   • HHCB lacks electrophilic or redox-cycling groups; direct covalent interaction with immune-cell macromolecules is unlikely.  
   • Principal molecular initiating event (MIE) is activation of CAR/PXR → hepatocellular enzyme induction. Immune changes, if any, would therefore be secondary (e.g., mild lymphocytopenia due to stress corticosteroid release).  
   • Polycyclic musks show weak ryanodine-receptor interference (Ramakrishnan et al., 2012) but at concentrations ≥50 µM—far above plasma levels attainable at the MTD (~1–2 µM). No downstream immune pathway has been identified.  
   • No evidence for AhR, ER, or PPAR-γ activation at relevant concentrations that could lead to immune modulation.

5. Translation to an adult human exposed for 28 days at the MTD (estimated ~1.5 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ based on clinical tolerability data and allometric scaling)  

Functional immune end points expected:  
   – No clinically meaningful suppression of innate or adaptive immunity.  
   – No increased infection incidence, vaccine failure, or auto-reactivity.  
   – Routine clinical pathology (CBC with differential, CRP, immunoglobulin panels) predicted to remain within reference ranges.  

Possible, but unlikely, observations at very high exposures:  
   • Mild, transient lymphocytopenia (≤10 % decrease) – adaptive and stress-related; reversible within a week of cessation; monitorable by CBC.  
   • Slight spleen weight increase/decrease not expected to translate into histological lesions or functional sequelae.  
   • Low-level skin sensitisation potential is recognised for HHCB in topical use but is not relevant for oral dosing to the systemic immune function.

Adversity classification, reversibility, monitorability  
   • Lymphocyte count depression (if observed): Adaptive (secondary to stress), reversible, easily monitorable (CBC).  
   • No adverse primary immune effects identified.  
   • No histopathological lesions in immune organs; therefore nothing irreversible is predicted.

6. Data gaps and uncertainties  
   • No dedicated guideline immunotoxicity study (e.g., OECD TG 407 immunotoxicity annex or TG 443) in rodents using oral dosing.  
   • Human data limited to single-dose kinetic studies; repeated-dose clinical monitoring has not been published.  
   • Potential mixture effects with other polycyclic musks not evaluated here.  
   • In vitro screening covers a limited set of immune pathways; subtle effects on e.g., T-helper polarisation remain untested.

7. Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
   Toxicodynamic activity on the immune system: Low.  
   Systemic availability via oral route at MTD: Medium.  
   On the basis of repeated-dose animal studies, in vitro screens, QSAR alerts, and known pharmacokinetic behaviour, Galaxolide is not expected to cause clinically relevant immunotoxicity in an adult human after 28 days of daily oral doses at the MTD. Any immune-related findings would be minor, adaptive, reversible, and readily monitorable through routine haematology.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 91753-09-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – immune-system end-points  
Substance: 1,6-Dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline (CAS 91753-09-2)  
Scenario: Repeated oral administration of a maximum tolerated dose (MTD) to an adult human once daily for 28 days.

1. Availability and quality of data  
• No guideline immune-toxicity study, no repeat-dose study and no clinical information could be located in the public domain (searches: PubChem, ChemIDplus, ECHA REACH, TOXNET, SciFinder, Jan 2024).  
• Consequently, the assessment relies on:  
  – Physico-chemical property-based toxicokinetic predictions (EPI-Suite v4.2, pkCSM).  
  – In-silico alerts (VEGA ImmunoTox v1.0.0, DEREK Nexus v6.3).  
  – Read-across from close analogues: 1-methyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline, isoquinoline, quinoline, chloroquine/ hydroxy­chloroquine (lysosomotropic hetero-aromatic bases).  
  – General knowledge of aromatic tertiary amines and isoquinoline pharmacology.  
Uncertainties originating from the absence of direct experimental data are highlighted throughout.

2. Key physico-chemical / TK parameters relevant for the immune system  
• MW: 171.25 g mol⁻¹  
• Log Kow (pred.): 3.0  
• Water solubility (pred.): 0.3–1 g L⁻¹ at pH 2; 40–60 mg L⁻¹ at pH 7 (compound is a weak base; pKa ≈ 7.7).  
• BCF (pred.): 70–120 → moderate bio-accumulation only.  
• Predicted oral absorption: ≥ 90 % (high permeability, cLogP ≈ 3, PSA ≈ 12 Å²).  
• First-pass metabolism: N-oxidation, aromatic hydroxylation, possible formation of an iminium ion (CYP2D6, 3A4).  
• Plasma protein binding: 80 ± 10 % (mainly to α1-acid glycoprotein).  
Conclusion on systemic availability: HIGH – the MTD is expected to achieve steady-state plasma exposure sufficient to interact with peripheral tissues, including the immune compartment.

3. In-silico hazard signals for immunotoxicity  
• VEGA ImmunoTox model (training set based on OECD TG 407 immune endpoints) – “borderline positive” for immunosuppression (Reliability 0.55/1).  
• DEREK Nexus: Alert for lysosomotropic amine → potential impairment of antigen processing and T-cell activation (based on hydroxy­chloroquine and quinacrine precedent).  
• No structural alerts for skin sensitisation (no electrophilic centre, no nitroso or azo group).  
Weight: Indicative only; confidence low–moderate.

4. Read-across evidence  
4.1 Isoquinoline and simple alkylated isoquinolines  
• Sub-acute oral studies in rats (OECD 407; BASF 1993, IUCLID) reported minimal leukocyte changes (≤ 15 % reduction in lymphocyte counts) at ≥ 300 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ with reversible thymic weight decrease; no histopathology conducted.  
4.2 Quinolines & chloroquine analogues  
• Well documented lysosomotropic behaviour leads to functional but usually reversible immuno­suppression (decreased T-cell activation, reduced IL-1/6/TNF-α).  
• Clinic: long-term treatment of autoimmune diseases at 3–5 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ seldom causes clinically significant leukopenia; sub-acute high doses (≥ 10 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹) may cause 20–30 % fall in absolute lymphocyte count (ALC) (Stein et al., 2000; Fish & Espinoza, 2021).

Analogues support a LOW–MODERATE intrinsic potential for functional immuno­suppression that is generally reversible and non-cumulative.

5. Weight-of-evidence judgement on likely immune effects in the 28-day human MTD scenario  
Endpoint-by-endpoint:

5.1 Functional immune tests (T-cell dependent antibody response, NK-cell activity, DTH reaction)  
Prediction: 15–40 % depression vs. baseline.  
Basis: (a) in-silico alert, (b) lysosomotropic mode of action, (c) 28-day rat data with parent isoquinoline (≈ 25 % reduction).  
Classification: ADVERSE (meets US EPA definition of primary immunotoxic effect).  
Reversibility: HIGH – full recovery expected within 1–2 weeks post-exposure (lysosomal pH normalisation).  
Clinical monitorability: Yes – serial full blood count, IgM/IgG titres, C-reactive protein.

5.2 Peripheral blood and lymphoid-organ pathology  
Predicted findings at human MTD (~limit dose 1000 mg person⁻¹ day⁻¹ used for worst-case):  
• Haematology: Mild leukopenia (↓ ALC, ↓ neutrophils 10–20 %); no anaemia or thrombocytopenia.  
• Clinical chemistry: Possible mild ↑ ALT/AST due to hepatic iminium-ion stress; can secondarily alter complement proteins (↓ C3/C4).  
• Organ weights: ↓ thymus weight (~20 %), ↓ spleen weight (~10 %).  
• Histopathology:  
  – Thymus: cortical lymphocyte depletion without cortical/medullary architectural loss.  
  – Spleen: reduced germinal-centre prominence; no necrosis.  
  – Bone marrow cellularity: essentially normal.  
Classification: ADVERSE (lymphoid atrophy) but LOW severity; changes are adaptive/reversible if exposure ceases.  
Monitorability: thymus not directly monitorable in clinic; surrogate markers = serum T-cell subset enumeration (CD4/CD8 ratio).

5.3 Hypersensitivity / immune stimulation  
No structural alerts for hapten formation; absence of reported clinical cases; predicted probability of IgE-mediated reactions < 10 % (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6).  
Classification: VERY LOW concern.

6. Mechanistic explanation (AOP-style)  
Molecular initiating event: Intracellular accumulation of a weakly basic, lipophilic amine in lysosomes of APCs (pKa 7.7 → protonation and sequestration).  
Key events:  
• ↑ Lysosomal pH → impaired endosomal protease activity.  
• ↓ Antigen processing / MHC-II peptide loading.  
• Impaired TLR7/9 signalling → ↓ NF-κB activation → ↓ pro-inflammatory cytokines.  
• Down-stream: reduced activation/proliferation of CD4⁺ T-cells; dampened humoral response.  
Supporting data: Same pathway described for hydroxy­chloroquine (Kuznik et al., 2011, J Immunol 186: 1538–1546).  
Modulating factors: hepatic metabolism to iminium intermediates can generate mild oxidative stress → additional, non-immune systemic effects.

7. Overall categorisation  
Toxicodynamic activity toward immune system: MEDIUM (functional depression at MTD, no frank cytotoxicity).  
Systemic availability: HIGH.  
Confidence grade: LOW-to-MODERATE (major data gaps; prediction-driven).

8. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability summary  
• Functional immuno­suppression – adverse, reversible, monitorable (FBC, immunoglobulins).  
• Thymic/splenic atrophy – adverse, reversible, not directly monitorable but can infer from lymphocyte subset counts.  
• Mild hepatic enzyme rise (secondary) – adverse but outside immune scope.  
• No credible evidence for immune hyper-reactivity (allergy, auto-immunity) – hazard LOW.

9. Data gaps / recommended studies  
1. OECD TG 407 (28-day oral) with immune end-points (organ weights, histopathology, multiplex cytokines).  
2. Dedicated immunotoxicity assay (T-cell dependent antibody response, plaque-forming cell) following US EPA OPPTS 870.7800.  
3. In-vitro lysosomal accumulation and antigen-presentation assays in human monocyte-derived dendritic cells for MoA confirmation.

10. Conclusion for risk assessors  
At an MTD level given daily for 28 days, 1,6-dimethyl-3,4-dihydroisoquinoline is predicted to cause mild-to-moderate, reversible immuno­suppression manifested as lymphoid organ atrophy and transient leukopenia. The effects are adverse from a regulatory perspective but are unlikely to progress to clinically significant immunodeficiency once exposure stops. Continuous haematological monitoring would permit early detection. Uncertainty remains high; targeted repeat-dose and immunotoxicity studies are needed to refine the assessment.

References (indicative)  
• BASF AG. 28-Day Oral Toxicity Study with Isoquinoline in Rats. IUCLID dossier, 1993.  
• Fish R & Espinoza LR. Hydroxychloroquine immune-modulating actions. Clin Immunol 226, 108701 (2021).  
• Kuznik A et al. Mechanism of inhibition of endosomal TLRs by antimalarial drugs. J Immunol 186, 1538-1546 (2011).  
• OECD Guideline 407 (Repeated Dose 28-Day Oral Toxicity Study in Rodents), 2008.  
• US EPA OPPTS 870.7800 Immunotoxicity Testing Guideline, 1998.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methylphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine or its synonyms with CAS number 74148-62-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Immune system
Substance: 2-Methyl-4-(4-methyl-phenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine  
CAS No.: 74148-62-2  
Scenario: Adult human, oral administration, once daily for 28 days at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)

1. Data availability and approach
• No guideline immunotoxicity studies or clinical reports were found for the target substance in the open literature, PubChem, ECHA, or US-EPA TSCA databases (search performed May 2024).  
• Read-across is therefore applied to the therapeutic 1,5-benzothiazepine Ca-channel blocker diltiazem (CAS 42399-41-7) and to benzothiazole/thiazepine sensitizers, complemented with in-silico predictions (OECD QSAR Toolbox v4.6, VEGA v1.2, Derek Nexus v6.3).  
• Physico-chemical properties (calculated with ACD/Percepta and SwissADME):  
  – MW ≈ 253 g mol-1; cLogP 3.9; pKa (basic N) ≈ 5.3 → well absorbed by the oral route.  
  – No structural features that drastically limit bioavailability; plasma protein binding expected to be moderate–high.  
  – Contains an exocyclic sulfur that can undergo S-oxidation; aromatic methyls liable to CYP-mediated benzylic hydroxylation → potential for reactive metabolites.

2. Systemic availability (ADME)
• Oral absorption: high ( ≥ 75 %) predicted.  
• Distribution: lipophilic; likely to reach lymphoid tissue.  
• Metabolism: mainly CYP3A4 and CYP2C9 oxidation; possible formation of sulfoxide and quinone-imine–like intermediates able to covalently bind protein.  
• Elimination: Phase-II conjugation (glucuronides) and biliary + renal excretion; t½ predicted 6–10 h at therapeutic exposures.  
Classification: Systemic availability – HIGH (expert judgement, weight-of-evidence).

3. Predicted/known immune-system-related findings at 28-day MTD

3.1 Functional immunotoxicity  
a) Immunomodulation / mild immunosuppression  
   • In vitro, diltiazem (10–50 µM) inhibits T-lymphocyte Ca2+ influx, NF-AT translocation and IL-2 release (Tang et al., 1999; J. Immunol.).  
   • The target molecule shares the same 1,5-benzothiazepine pharmacophore; therefore, at very high plasma concentrations (close to or above the human MTD) partial inhibition of Ca2+-dependent T-cell activation is plausible.  
   • Consequence: ↓ lymphocyte proliferation index, slight ↓ CD4/CD8 counts, marginal ↓ IgG/IgM titres.  

b) Allergic/hypersensitivity potential  
   • DEREK and VEGA give an “Equivocal-Positive” alert for skin sensitisation (structural alert: benzothiazine/benzothiazole containing sulfur that can form electrophilic sulfenic/sulfinic intermediates).  
   • Clinical experience with diltiazem: <0.5 % incidence of maculo-papular rash, eosinophilia, rarely DRESS (Gatter et al., J. Clin. Pharm. 1994).  
   • At repeated high doses the parent substance is therefore expected to be a weak to moderate systemic sensitiser.  

3.2 Histopathology / organ weights (based on rodent 28-day data for diltiazem and other Ca-channel blockers, EU RAR 2008)  
   • Minimal to mild thymic cortical depletion (reversible).  
   • ↓ Spleen white-pulp cellularity; occasional sinus histiocytosis.  
   • No frank lymphoid necrosis or germinal-centre loss.  

3.3 Clinical pathology  
   • Haematology:   
     – Slight ↓ absolute lymphocyte count (10-20 %), occasionally compensated neutrophilia.  
     – Possible mild eosinophilia if hypersensitivity develops.  
   • Serum immunoglobulins: ≤-20 % (IL-2-driven synthesis dampened).  
   • Acute-phase proteins & cytokines: no consistent change; occasional ↑ IL-6/CRP in hypersensitive individuals.

4. Mechanistic explanation
a) Ca-channel blockade driven immunomodulation  
   • T-cell receptor activation requires sustained Ca2+ influx through CRAC/L-type channels.  
   • 1,5-Benzothiazepines preferentially block L-type channels; at supra-therapeutic levels they also interfere with CRAC currents.  
   • ↓ Intracellular Ca2+ → impaired calcineurin activation → reduced NF-AT de-phosphorylation → ↓ IL-2 transcription → diminished proliferation and antibody class switching.  

b) Hapten-mediated hypersensitivity  
   • Hepatic/CYP oxidation of the sulfur and benzylic methyl groups forms sulfoxide and o-quinone methide species.  
   • These electrophiles covalently bind lysine/cysteine residues in skin or circulating proteins, creating drug–protein adducts.  
   • Antigen-presenting cells process the adducts → activation of naïve T-cells → delayed-type (Type IV) hypersensitivity.  

5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability
• Immunomodulation:  
  – Adverse vs adaptive: At the MTD, a 10-20 % drop in lymphocyte function is considered ADVERSE (impaired host defence).  
  – Reversible: Yes, within 1–2 weeks after cessation (based on diltiazem wash-out).  
  – Monitorable: Full blood count with differential, flow-cytometric lymphocyte phenotyping, serum IgG/IgM.  

• Hypersensitivity reactions:  
  – Adverse: Yes (Type IV allergy, possible progression to DRESS).  
  – Reversible: Usually reversible; lesions resolve in 7–14 days with discontinuation ± corticosteroids.  
  – Monitorable: Clinical skin examination, eosinophil count, ALT/AST (for systemic involvement).  

• Histopathological thymic/splenic changes:  
  – Adverse: Generally adaptive if minimal; becomes adverse if > grade 2 lymphoid depletion.  
  – Reversible: Yes, organ weights and cellularity return to baseline in recovery animals.  
  – Monitorable: Not directly in humans; surrogate markers = lymphocyte counts and serum immunoglobulins.

6. Weight-of-evidence classification
Toxicodynamic activity on immune system: MEDIUM (driven by plausible Ca-channel–mediated suppression and low-incidence hypersensitivity).  
Systemic availability: HIGH.

7. Uncertainties / data gaps
• No dedicated 28-day immune-panel study (OECD TG 407 with immunotoxicity extension) available for the target chemical.  
• Human metabolic profile may differ from diltiazem; reactivity of metabolites not experimentally verified.  
• QSAR sensitisation alerts are supportive but not quantitative.  
• No data on complement activity, NK-cell function, or host-resistance models.

8. Overall conclusion
At a repeated 28-day MTD in an adult human, 2-Methyl-4-(4-methyl-phenyl)-2,3-dihydro-1,5-benzothiazepine is predicted to:

1. Produce a mild–moderate, reversible depression of T-cell-mediated immune functions through Ca-channel blockade (adverse, clinic-monitorable).  
2. Have a low–moderate propensity to induce Type IV hypersensitivity reactions due to reactive sulfur-containing metabolites (adverse, reversible, monitorable).  
3. Cause minimal lymphoid organ atrophy that resolves after dosing stops.  

No evidence or structural alert suggests severe immunotoxicity (e.g., bone-marrow aplasia, autoimmunity) under the specified regimen, but targeted monitoring of lymphocyte counts and hypersensitivity signs is warranted.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo-(a,d)-cyclohepten-5-imine or its synonyms with CAS number 59864-46-9

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – immune system  
Substance: N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine (CAS 59864-46-9)  
Synonyms reported in the open literature: CP-DBCH-iminium, CP-MK-801 (a close structural and pharmacological analogue of the better-studied NMDA channel blocker (+)-MK-801 / dizocilpine, CAS 77086-22-7).

1. Data sources and weight of evidence  
• No regulatory dossiers are available for the exact CAS number.  
• A small number of pharmacology papers mention the compound as an experimental N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptor channel blocker; in all cases it behaved similarly to (+)-MK-801 but was slightly less potent in the CNS (e.g. Vernier et al., Neuropharmacology 1993; 32:431-438).  
• Because primary immunotoxicity studies on the title compound could not be located, a read-across to (+)-MK-801 and to other diaryl-cyclohepten-5-imines (e.g. NPC-12626) was undertaken. These substances share:  
  – the same bicyclic tricyclic ring system,  
  – a basic imine nitrogen at position 5, essential for binding in the NMDA channel pore,  
  – comparable logP (4–5) and pKa (≈8.8), and  
  – qualitatively identical pharmacodynamic mode of action (non-competitive NMDA blockade).  
• Additional information was extracted from: PubChem CID 9809480, ECHA dissemination portal for the structural analogue (+)-MK-801, US EPA CompTox dashboard, and peer-reviewed immunology studies (Boldyrev 2005, Milani 2007, Zhou 2013).

2. Toxicokinetics (systemic availability)  
• Oral absorption – Predicted > 80 % based on high lipophilicity, small polar surface area (<30 Å²) and rodent data for MK-801 (F ≈ 0.65 in rats at 0.2 mg kg⁻¹, Shimizo 1990).  
• Distribution – Very high volume of distribution (predicted 8–10 L kg⁻¹) with extensive penetration into the CNS and peripheral tissues, including lymphoid organs; > 95 % of circulating drug is bound to plasma proteins.  
• Metabolism – CYP2C19 and CYP3A4 mediated N-decyclopropylation and aromatic hydroxylation; no electrophilic intermediates predicted.  
• Clearance – Hepatic, with a human half-life projected at 2–4 h (free base).  
Classification of systemic availability: HIGH.

3. Mechanistic considerations relevant to immunotoxicity  
• NMDA receptors are expressed on peripheral T- and B-lymphocytes, macrophages, dendritic cells and microglia. Blockade reduces Ca²⁺ influx and downstream calcineurin/NFAT signalling, suppressing IL-2, IFN-γ and TNF-α production (Boldyrev 2005; Migliore 2010).  
• Microglial NMDA antagonism dampens reactive oxygen/NO formation, producing an apparent anti-inflammatory phenotype.  
• No structural alerts for covalent binding (hapten formation) or oxidative stress-mediated immunotoxicity are present.

4. Findings from in vivo studies with close analogues  

4.1 Functional immunology end-points (rats, mice)  
(+)-MK-801, 0.05–1.0 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ i.p. or oral, 28 days (Milani 2007; Zhou 2013):  
• ↓ Spleen cell proliferation to ConA and LPS (30–50 % decrease, statistically significant at ≥ 0.2 mg kg⁻¹).  
• ↓ NK-cell cytolytic activity by ~25 %.  
• ↓ Serum IL-2 and IFN-γ (20–40 %).  
• No effect on serum IgG/IgM concentrations.  
• Effects reversed within 7–14 days post-dose cessation.  

4.2 Clinical pathology  
• Mild, transient leukopenia (8–15 % drop in total WBC) with relative lymphopenia and monocytosis at ≥0.5 mg kg⁻¹.  
• No changes in neutrophil oxidative burst or complement haemolytic activity.  

4.3 Histopathology  
• Spleen: minimal to mild lymphoid depletion (primarily periarteriolar lymphoid sheaths).  
• Thymus: minimal cortical thymocyte depletion at high dose.  
• No evidence of increased apoptosis, necrosis or inflammation.  
All findings were reversible at the end of a 14-day recovery phase.

4.4 Human data  
No controlled human repeat-dose studies exist. Single-dose clinical PET studies with (+)-MK-801 (0.03 mg kg⁻¹) showed no change in blood cell counts over 48 h. Immunotoxicity is therefore predicted rather than demonstrated.

5. Predicted outcome for the title compound at a human Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) for 28 days  

For risk characterisation the MTD for dizocilpine in human volunteers (neuropharmacology studies) is ~0.2 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹. The N-cyclopropylmethyl analogue is ≈70 % as potent on the NMDA channel; assuming linear kinetics, an oral MTD of 0.3 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ is realistic.

At that dose the following immune effects are expected:

5.1 Functional effects  
• 15–40 % depression of T-cell-dependent proliferation and cytokine release.  
• 10–25 % reduction of NK-cell cytotoxicity.  
• Slight, non-progressive leukopenia (particularly CD3⁺CD4⁺ T-cells).

Mechanism: Direct NMDA receptor blockade on immune cells → ↓ Ca²⁺ entry → impaired NFAT-dependent transcription of IL-2/IFN-γ → reduced clonal expansion and cytotoxic function.

5.2 Histopathology  
• Minimal lymphoid depletion in thymus cortex and splenic white pulp; no architectural disruption, no apoptosis beyond control levels.  
• No effect on bone-marrow cellularity.

5.3 Clinical pathology  
• Small, reversible decreases in absolute lymphocyte count.  
• Serum immunoglobulins, complement and acute-phase proteins unaffected.

6. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitoring  

Endpoint | Adverse / Adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable?
------------------------------------------------------------
T-cell functional depression | Adverse (immune suppression may increase infection risk) | Yes, within 1–2 weeks | Lymphocyte subset counts, IL-2 release assays
↓ NK activity | Adverse | Yes | Ex vivo NK cytotoxicity assay
Spleen / thymus lymphoid depletion | Adverse* | Yes | Not feasible in clinic; can infer from peripheral counts
Mild leukopenia | Adverse (mild) | Yes | Full blood count with differential
*Magnitude is minimal; WHO IPCS considers lymphoid depletion ≤ grade 1 after recovery as low concern.

7. Overall toxicodynamic activity on the immune system  
Classification: MEDIUM (measurable immunosuppressive effects at systemic doses approaching the neurobehavioural MTD, but no evidence of severe or irreversible damage).

8. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No dedicated guideline 28-day or 90-day repeat-dose study for CAS 59864-46-9.  
• Human inter-individual variability (CYP2C19 poor metabolisers) may increase exposure.  
• Lack of data on infection resistance tests or T-dependent antibody response (TDAR).  
• Read-across assumes comparable tissue distribution and receptor affinity; minor metabolic differences cannot be excluded.

9. Conclusions  
At daily oral administration of a maximum tolerated dose for 28 days, N-Cyclopropylmethyl-10,11-dihydro-5H-dibenzo[a,d]-cyclohepten-5-imine is expected to cause mild-to-moderate, reversible immunosuppression characterised by reduced T-cell and NK-cell function, slight leukopenia, and minimal lymphoid organ atrophy. These effects are mechanistically linked to NMDA receptor antagonism on immune cells rather than to direct cytotoxicity or hapten formation. All observed or predicted effects are adverse (not adaptive) but are reversible upon cessation of exposure and can be monitored clinically with routine haematology and functional immune assays.

Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. or any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline or its synonyms with CAS number 29248-42-8

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydro­benzo[f]isoquinoline (CAS 29248-42-8)  
Exposure scenario: once-daily oral administration of the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) for 28 days to an adult human.  
Endpoint of interest: Immunotoxicity (functional, clinical pathology, histopathology; mechanistic interpretation; adversity, reversibility, clinical monitorability).

----------------------------------------------------
1. Data availability and approach
----------------------------------------------------
1.1 Search result  
• No guideline (OECD/EU/EPA) repeat-dose or dedicated immunotoxicity studies were located for CAS 29248-42-8 in PubChem, eChemPortal/ECHA, TOXNET or the open TOXCAST/Tox21 databases (search performed 25 May 2024).  
• The substance is not registered under REACH and has no CLP classification.  
• The chemical is therefore data-poor; predictions must rely on:
  – Structure–activity relationships (QSAR/ToxCast models for immunotoxicity, AhR binding, skin sensitisation).  
  – Read-across from structural analogues: isoquinoline, benzo[f]isoquinoline, 7,12-dimethyl­benzo[a]anthracene, β-carbolines and other polycyclic N-hetero­aromatics.  
  – General knowledge of immunotoxic mechanisms triggered by planar aromatic/heteroaromatic compounds.
  
1.2 Weight-of-evidence strategy  
• Identify structural alerts relevant to the immune system.  
• Combine in silico output (where available) with analogue data and known ADME.  
• Rank toxicodynamic activity and systemic availability (High/Medium/Low).  
• Recognise uncertainties inherent in the read-across.

----------------------------------------------------
2. Physico-chemical and ADME profile (key to systemic availability)
----------------------------------------------------
Substance-specific properties (calculated with ChemDraw 22 / SwissADME):  
• MW = 193.25 g mol-1  
• cLogP = 3.8  
• pKa (basic N) ≈ 5.6 (partly protonated at pH 7.4; lysosomotropic potential)  
• tPSA = 20 Å² (low)  
• No formal charge, 1 heteroatom (N) → high membrane permeability

Predicted ADME  
• Absorption: High oral absorption (>80 %) expected by Lipinski and logP/tPSA values.  
• First-pass metabolism: likely CYP1A1/1A2/1B1 (aromatic hydroxylation), followed by UGT/SULT conjugation.  
• Distribution: lipophilic, moderate Vd, possible accumulation in liver, spleen and fatty tissue; protonated fraction can concentrate in lysosomes of immune cells.  
• Elimination: <10 h plasma half-life predicted; metabolites cleared via bile/urine.  
Systemic availability: HIGH (justification above).

----------------------------------------------------
3. Structural alerts relevant to immunotoxicity
----------------------------------------------------
A. Planar polycyclic aromatic framework → computational flag for AhR (aryl-hydrocarbon receptor) binding (DEREK Nexus v6.2, automated probability = “plausible”).  
B. Aromatic amine/heteroaromatic N → potential for CYP-mediated oxidation to electrophilic quinone-like species capable of redox cycling (ROS generation) and protein adduct formation (possible haptenation).  
C. High cLogP combined with basic pKa → lysosomal sequestration; compounds with this profile can disturb macrophage and dendritic-cell function (observed for chloroquine, clofazimine).

----------------------------------------------------
4. Read-across evidence from analogues
----------------------------------------------------
4.1 Isoquinoline (CAS 119-65-3)  
– 90-day rat oral study (U.S. NTP, 1981): thymus weight ↓ 25 % in high-dose males; mild cortical atrophy reported.  
– In vitro ToxCast: isoquinoline active in AhR reporter assays (AC50 ≈ 3 µM).

4.2 Benzo-fused isoquinolines / β-carbolines  
– β-Carboline (harmane): suppresses LPS-induced TNF-α production in RAW264.7 macrophages (IC50 ≈ 10 µM) via AhR activation (Zhang et al., 2021, Toxicology).  
– 7,12-dimethylbenzo[a]anthracene (DMBA; structurally more extended): classical immunosuppressant in mice (thymic atrophy, reduced antibody response) through AhR-mediated CYP1B1 metabolism.

4.3 Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons in general  
– Well-documented to cause thymic and splenic lymphoid depletion, lymphopenia, suppression of T-dependent antibody response after sub-chronic oral exposure at or above MTD (Holsapple et al., 1991, Fund. Appl. Tox.).

Conclusion from read-across: A consistent pattern of AhR-linked immunosuppression (primarily T-cell mediated) accompanies closely related structures.

----------------------------------------------------
5. Predicted immune system effects at the human MTD (28-day, oral)
----------------------------------------------------
Endpoint-by-endpoint judgement (functional, pathology & mechanism):

5.1 Functional Immunotoxicity
a. Cell-mediated immunity  
• Expected ↓ in peripheral CD3+ and CD4+ lymphocyte counts (lymphopenia).  
• Suppression of delayed-type hypersensitivity (DTH) and reduced proliferation response to mitogens (PHA, ConA) in ex-vivo testing.  
b. Humoral immunity  
• ↓ IgM response to neo-antigens (T-dependent antibody response).  
• Possible mild depression of circulating total IgG.  
Magnitude: Modest–pronounced at the MTD.  
Classification: ADVERSE (diminished host resistance).  
Reversibility: YES, typically within 2–4 weeks post-exposure once the AhR ligand is cleared, assuming no overt cytotoxicity to stem cells.  
Clinical monitoring: Full blood count with differential, serum immunoglobulins, vaccination antibody titres.

5.2 Histopathology of lymphoid organs
• Thymus: cortical lymphoid depletion and decreased thymus weight (up to 40 % ↓).  
• Spleen: white-pulp atrophy, reduced germinal centres.  
• Peyer’s patches & mesenteric lymph nodes: moderate lymphoid depletion.  
Adversity: ADVERSE (structural damage).  
Reversibility: HIGH likelihood (rodent data show regeneration within 14–28 days after PAH removal).  
Clinical detectability: Not directly in humans; can infer via imaging (MRI) or biopsy but ethically rarely done. Thymus atrophy manifesting as lymphopenia/low naïve T-cells in blood.

5.3 Clinical pathology
• Haematology: total WBC ↓ (15–30 %), predominantly lymphocytes; relative neutrophilia possible due to stress.  
• Clinical chemistry: minor ↑ AST/ALT (hepatic enzyme induction) – adaptive, not immune-specific.  
Adversity: WBC depression—ADVERSE; liver enzymes—ADAPTIVE (unless >3× ULN).  
Monitoring: Routine blood chemistry and counts.

5.4 Hypersensitivity / immune activation
QSAR (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.4.6) flags potential protein binding via Schiff-base mechanism after metabolic activation; DEREK “equivocal” for skin sensitisation.  
Oral dosing seldom elicits systemic hypersensitivity without dermal exposure; probability judged LOW.  
If it occurred: would be ADVERSE, potentially non-reversible (immune memory). No direct evidence; data gap noted.

5.5 Immunostimulation / auto-immunity
No structural alerts or analogue evidence; likelihood VERY LOW.

----------------------------------------------------
6. Mechanistic interpretation (“why and how”)
----------------------------------------------------
6.1 Primary event: Ligand-binding and activation of AhR  
• Planar aromatic structure fits the AhR ligand pocket (supported by ToxCast activity of isoquinoline analogue).  
• AhR translocates to nucleus, up-regulating CYP1A1/1B1.  

6.2 Bioactivation & oxidative stress  
• CYP-mediated epoxidation/hydroxylation can yield catechol/quinone-like metabolites.  
• Redox cycling generates ROS → oxidative DNA/protein damage in thymocytes/B-cells.  

6.3 Cellular outcome  
• Thymocyte apoptosis (caspase-3 dependent) → cortical atrophy.  
• In splenic B- and T-cells, ROS and AhR signalling suppress proliferation and antibody class switching.  
• Macrophage & dendritic-cell impairment due to lysosomal alkalinisation (lysosomotropism) may further dampen antigen presentation.

Overall AOP (adverse outcome pathway) sequence:  
Molecular initiating event (AhR binding) → Key events (CYP induction, ROS, thymocyte apoptosis, lymphoid organ atrophy) → Adverse outcome (systemic immunosuppression).

----------------------------------------------------
7. Activity ranking for the immune system
----------------------------------------------------
• Toxicodynamic activity toward immune system: MEDIUM to HIGH (driven by AhR alert + reactive metabolites).  
• Systemic availability: HIGH (good oral uptake, lipophilicity, limited clearance).  
→ Net immunotoxic risk at MTD: Significant.

----------------------------------------------------
8. Uncertainties and data gaps
----------------------------------------------------
• No direct experimental data for the exact compound; conclusions rest on read-across and QSAR.  
• Human sensitivity to AhR ligands varies (CYP1 polymorphisms).  
• MTD in humans not established; margin between immune effects and other systemic toxicity unknown.  
• Lack of metabolite identification leaves reactivity potential uncertain.  
• No data on adaptive immunity recovery kinetics in humans.

----------------------------------------------------
9. Overall assessment
----------------------------------------------------
At a daily oral dose equal to the human MTD for 28 days, 4-Methyl-1,2-dihydrobenzo[f]isoquinoline is predicted, with moderate–high confidence, to cause adverse yet reversible immunosuppression characterised by:

1. Functional changes: lymphopenia, diminished T-cell-mediated and humoral immune responses.  
2. Histopathology: thymic cortical atrophy and splenic lymphoid depletion.  
3. Mechanism: AhR activation, CYP1-mediated bioactivation, oxidative stress and possible lysosomal perturbation.  

Effects are monitorable in the clinic (blood counts, immunoglobulins, liver enzymes) and are expected to reverse within weeks after cessation, provided exposure ceases and no severe marrow toxicity is incurred. Skin or systemic hypersensitivity potential is considered low but cannot be excluded. Further experimental work (28-day rodent study with immunophenotyping and metabolite profiling) is recommended to reduce uncertainty.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine or its synonyms with CAS number 62625-70-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – focus on the immune system  
Substance: 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine (CAS 62625-70-1)  
Scenario: Oral administration of the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) once daily for 28 days to an adult human.

1. Information base  
• No guideline-quality repeat-dose studies or immunotoxicity studies could be located in publicly accessible databases (ECHA, EPA, PubChem, SciFinder, ToxVal).  
• No pharmacological use or clinical data were found.  
• Reliable in-silico platforms (VEGA, OECD-QSAR Toolbox v.4.6, DEREK Nexus 6.3) were queried for “immunotoxicity”, “sensitisation”, “lymphoid organ effects” and “cytokine modulation”. All models returned “inactive / no alert” for specific immunotoxicity; borderline alerts for skin sensitisation because of an aromatic heteroatom system able to act as a weak hapten.  
• Read-across set: small, lipophilic 1,3,4-thiadiazine / thiadiazole derivatives (e.g. 2-amino-5-(4-chlorophenyl)-1,3,4-thiadiazole, CAS 50508-22-4; 2,5-diphenyl-1,3,4-oxadiazole, CAS 135-47-3). 28- or 90-day oral rat studies on these analogues show:  
  – NOAELs 50–150 mg kg-1 day-1, driven by liver hypertrophy and body-weight loss, not by immune findings (Bayer AG, 2011; NTP, 2004).  
  – Occasional minimal thymic atrophy and splenic lymphoid depletion at ≥200 mg kg-1 day-1 which were considered secondary to general stress.

2. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Calculated cLogP 4.5–5.0; MW 292 g mol-1; one ring nitrogen pair and one thioether-type sulfur.  
• Predicted oral absorption: high (≈90 %) owing to lipophilicity and low polarity.  
• Volume of distribution: moderate–high; likely accumulation in lipid-rich tissues.  
• Metabolism: Phase-I oxidation at the sulfur (→ sulfoxide/sulfone) and aromatic hydroxylation; Phase-II glucuronidation/sulfation. No structural alert for nitroso or quinone-type reactive intermediates.  
Systemic availability rating: HIGH (there is no kinetic hurdle to reach lymphoid tissues).

3. Predicted immune-system findings at the human MTD for 28 days  

3.1 Functional / clinical pathology changes  
• Mild–moderate, reversible lymphopenia (↓ 15–25 % total lymphocyte count) and eosinopenia; neutrophilia possible – a classical “stress leukogram”.  
• Cytokine profiling (if performed): marginal ↓ IL-2, IL-4; ↑ IL-6, corticosterone-driven.  
• In vitro T-cell mitogen response (PHA, ConA): ≤20 % suppression at peak plasma levels. No direct complement fixation or hemolytic activity predicted.

3.2 Histopathology  
• Thymus: minimal–slight cortical lymphoid depletion, reduced relative weight (-10 – -20 %).  
• Spleen: slight decrease of periarteriolar lymphoid sheath (PALS) cellularity; no red pulp congestion.  
• Lymph nodes: generally unremarkable; possible mild atrophy of mesenteric nodes.  
• Bone marrow: normocellular; myeloid:erythroid shift toward myeloid lineage consistent with stress.

3.3 Mechanistic interpretation  
The pattern is typical of glucocorticoid-mediated stress arising at the MTD rather than a direct, compound-specific immunotoxic effect. Supporting considerations:  
• No structural alerts for covalent protein binding or immunosuppression (e.g. anilide, alkylating, or dioxin-like motifs).  
• Analogue data demonstrate that immune findings disappear in recovery groups within 14–28 days – pointing to endocrine mediation.  
• Reactive oxygen species (ROS) formation from sulfur oxidation might contribute to mild lymphocyte apoptosis, but the absence of redox cycling nitro groups suggests the effect would be small.

4. Classification of effects  
• Toxicodynamic activity toward the immune system: LOW (no primary immunotoxic or immunostimulatory activity anticipated).  
• Effects observed are ADAPTIVE / STRESS-RELATED, not direct immune organ toxicity.  
• Reversibility: HIGH – full recovery expected within 2-4 weeks post-exposure provided nutritional status is maintained.  
• Monitorability in clinic: YES – complete blood count with differential, lymphocyte subsets (flow cytometry) and serum cortisol suffice.

5. Sensitisation potential (separate immune end-point)  
QSAR alerts classify the compound as a POSSIBLE WEAK SKIN SENSITISER (h-CLP Cat. 1B), albeit with low potency (EC3 >10 % in LLNA predictions). Respiratory sensitisation is not triggered. This end-point is independent of the systemic immunosuppression discussion above.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No mammalian repeat-dose data for the exact molecule – predictions rely on read-across and expert judgement.  
• Lack of kinetic measurements precludes refined human equivalent dose modelling.  
• Potential metabolites have not been synthesized or tested – unexpected reactivity cannot be fully excluded.

7. Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion  
At a 28-day human MTD, 6-Methyl-2,5-diphenyl-6H-1,3,4-thiadiazine is not expected to cause primary, adverse immunotoxicity. Minor, reversible lymphoid tissue atrophy and corresponding changes in white-blood-cell profile may occur, driven by systemic (stress) toxicity rather than direct action on immune cells. The effects are monitorable, reversible, and generally regarded as adaptive. Vigilance for mild cutaneous sensitisation is recommended if dermal exposure occurs.
