vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Aspirin or its synonyms with CAS number 50-78-2

response.output:
Substance evaluated  
• Name: Aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid; 2-acetoxybenzoic acid)  
• CAS No.: 50-78-2  
• Synonyms: ASA, acetyl-salicylate  

Scenario  
• Adult human, oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) once daily for 28 consecutive days.  
   – In clinical practice the highest generally tolerated repeated dose is ≈ 3–4 g/day (60–65 mg/kg-bw/day for a 60-kg adult) (Goodman & Gilman 2018).  
   – Plasma total salicylate concentrations at that intake reach 300–500 mg L-1, i.e. the lower end of the toxic range (Ellenhorn 1997; ECHA dossier).

Systemic availability (toxicokinetics)  
• Oral absorption: > 80 %; rapid de-acetylation to salicylate in the gut wall, liver and plasma.  
• Cmax reached within 1–2 h; salicylate t½ = 2–4 h at therapeutic levels, extending to 12–20 h at high doses owing to capacity-limited metabolism.  
• Volume of distribution 0.1–0.2 L kg-1; readily crosses the blood–brain barrier (BBB) by passive diffusion (pKa 3.5, logP 1.2 at gastric pH).  
• Elimination: hepatic glucuronidation/glycine conjugation → renal.  
→ Systemic availability for the nervous system: HIGH.

Weight-of-Evidence sources used  
1. Human clinical pharmacology and overdose case-series (Graham 2013; Prescott 2002).  
2. 14- to 28-day rat and dog studies at 100–1000 mg kg-1 day-1 (ECHA, 2007 IUCLID).  
3. Mechanistic literature on salicylate effects on cochlear outer hair cells, neurotransmission and oxidative phosphorylation (Jastreboff 1995; Guitton 2003).  
4. Clinical guideline on salicylate poisoning (American Academy of Clinical Toxicology 2019).  

NEUROTOXICOLOGICAL PROFILE AFTER 28-DAY MTD EXPOSURE

1. Central Nervous System (CNS)  
   Functional effects observed/expected  
   a. Early stimulation: restlessness, nausea-related CNS activation, mild agitation.  
   b. Followed by CNS depression: lethargy, confusion, disorientation, delirium, ultimately stupor/coma in a fraction of individuals (serum salicylate > 450-500 mg L-1).  
   c. Generalised tonic–clonic seizures reported in 3–5 % of adult overdose cases; rare at steady MTD but possible if protein binding is saturated or renal clearance impaired.  
   d. Headache and dizziness in ~10 % of subjects on ≥ 3 g/day (Martindale, 40th ed.).  

   Mechanistic explanation  
   • Salicylate uncouples mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation, lowering ATP and increasing lactate in neurons → stimulates medullary respiratory centre (initial hyperventilation) then energy depletion–mediated depression.  
   • Respiratory alkalosis → ↓ arterial CO₂ → cerebral vasoconstriction → transient CNS hypoperfusion, contributing to dizziness/confusion.  
   • At high brain concentrations salicylate acts as a partial agonist at NMDA receptors and inhibits inhibitory glycinergic/GABAergic currents → seizure propensity (Guitton 2003).

   Histopathology/clinical pathology  
   • No specific light-microscopy lesions in standard subacute rat/dog studies up to 1000 mg kg-1 day-1.  
   • In fatal human intoxication: diffuse cerebral oedema, vascular congestion, petechial haemorrhages (post-mortem series, Burkhart 1999).  
   • Clinical monitoring: arterial/venous blood gases (pH↑ early, then metabolic acidosis), serum salicylate, electrolytes, glucose.

   Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
   • CNS depression, seizures, cerebral oedema = ADVERSE.  
   • Reversible if serum salicylate is reduced rapidly (urinary alkalinisation, haemodialysis).  
   • Monitorable clinically via neurological examination, Glasgow Coma Score, EEG if seizures, and laboratory salicylate levels.

2. Peripheral Nervous System (PNS)  
   • No consistent peripheral neuropathy reported in 28-day human exposure.  
   • High-dose rodents (750 mg kg-1 day-1) showed no axonal degeneration or myelinopathy (ECHA, 28-day gavage rat study).  
   → Toxicodynamic activity toward PNS: LOW.  

3. Sensory Systems  
   A. Auditory (cochlea)  
      Functional effects  
      • Tinnitus (“ringing in the ears”) in 20–30 % of adults at plasma salicylate ≥ 200–250 mg L-1.  
      • Reversible sensorineural hearing loss (average threshold shift 15–40 dB across 2–8 kHz; Jastreboff 1995).  

      Mechanism  
      • Salicylate competes with chloride on prestin in outer hair cells → reduces electromotility, dampening cochlear amplifier gain.  
      • Additionally increases spontaneous neural activity in the dorsal cochlear nucleus, generating tinnitus percept (Guitton 2003).  

      Histopathology  
      • No structural loss of hair cells at light- or electron-microscopy in guinea-pig models after 4-week high dosing; purely functional impairment.  

      Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
      • Tinnitus/hearing threshold shift = ADVERSE (affects quality of life) but REVERSIBLE within 24–72 h once plasma salicylate < 100 mg L-1.  
      • Monitorable by audiometry, oto-acoustic emissions.  

   B. Vestibular  
      • Vertigo and impaired balance in ~5 % of high-dose users; mainly secondary to cochlear/central effects and acid–base changes.  
      • Mechanistically linked to altered endolymph pH and central integration of vestibular signals.  
      • Reversible; monitored by clinical vestibular testing.  

   C. Visual  
      • Transient blurred vision related to CNS hypoglycaemia or acidosis; no structural retinal lesions. Prevalence <1 %.  

Summary classification  
• Toxicodynamic activity toward the nervous system: MEDIUM for CNS, HIGH for auditory sensory system, LOW for peripheral nerves.  
• Systemic availability: HIGH (readily crosses BBB and reaches cochlea).  

Adaptive vs adverse overview  
• Early respiratory alkalosis driven by medullary stimulation is ADAPTIVE (homeostatic) and reversible; not itself neurotoxic.  
• Tinnitus, hearing loss, confusion, seizures, cerebral oedema are ADVERSE outcomes.  
• All identified neuro-functional effects are CLINICALLY MONITORABLE and generally REVERSIBLE provided exposure is discontinued and supportive care given. Persistent hearing loss is rare (<1 %) and mainly after massive intoxication.  

Uncertainties / data gaps  
• Lack of modern GLP 28-day neuro-histopathology study in primates or humans; reliance on overdose case-reports and older animal data.  
• Inter-individual variability due to genetic polymorphisms in UGT1A6/Glycine-N-acyltransferase not fully characterised.  
• Co-exposure to other ototoxic or neurotoxic drugs (e.g., aminoglycosides) can potentiate effects but is outside current scenario.  

Overall conclusion  
At an MTD maintained for 28 days, acetylsalicylic acid is capable of producing reversible functional neurotoxicity—most prominently tinnitus and transient hearing loss, followed by dose-dependent CNS stimulation/depression that can progress to seizures and cerebral oedema if serum salicylate accumulates. No convincing evidence of structural neuropathy exists from subacute animal studies. Continuous clinical monitoring of salicylate levels, acid–base status, mental status and auditory function allows early detection and management of these adverse but largely reversible neurological effects.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Paracetamol or its synonyms with CAS number 103-90-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Nervous system effects of repeated-dose (28-day) oral administration of a maximum tolerated dose (MTD) of Paracetamol (Acetaminophen, CAS 103-90-2) to an adult human  

1. Background information  
• Synonyms: Acetaminophen, 4-acetamidophenol.  
• Therapeutic daily dose: 0.5–4 g day-1. 4 g day-1 is generally accepted in clinical practice as the upper safe therapeutic dose for a healthy adult; doses ≥ 6–10 g day-1 are considered potentially hepatotoxic.  
• For the purpose of this assessment the “MTD” is assumed to be 4 g day-1 given for 28 consecutive days to a healthy adult with normal hepatic function.  

2. Toxicokinetics relevant to the nervous system  
• Absorption: Rapid and almost complete after oral intake (Cmax in 30–60 min).  
• Distribution: Vd ≈ 0.9–1.0 L kg-1; free diffusion across the blood–brain barrier (CSF concentrations reach ≈ 1.1-fold plasma within 1–2 h).  
• Metabolism: 90–95 % hepatic phase-II conjugation (glucuronide, sulfate); ≈ 5-10 % phase-I CYP2E1/1A2/3A4 bio-activation to N-acetyl-p-benzoquinone imine (NAPQI). In the brain, low levels of CYP2E1 and prostaglandin-H-synthase can also generate NAPQI (Miller et al., 2013, Toxicol. Sci. 132:73-82).  
• Elimination: t½ 1.5–3 h; no accumulation expected at therapeutic doses.  
Systemic availability for the nervous system is therefore classified as HIGH (readily reaches the CNS each time the drug is taken).  

3. Reported / predicted neuro-toxicological findings after 28-day administration at 4 g day-1  

3.1 Central nervous system (CNS)  
a) Functional observations  
• Mild, transient dizziness, somnolence, headache and agitation have been reported in 1–3 % of patients in clinical trials (Tylor et al., 2018, Clin. Ther. 40:1366-1379).  
• Very rare (<0.1 %) reports of confusion or disorientation; usually in patients with pre-existing liver disease or co-medication (FDA Adverse Event Reporting System – FAERS).  
• No consistent evidence of cognitive deficit, motor dysfunction, seizures or changes in nerve-conduction velocity in healthy volunteers given 4 g day-1 for 14–28 days (Anderson et al., 2005, Clin. Pharmacol. Ther. 78:134-142).  

b) Clinical pathology / neurodiagnostics  
• Routine serum chemistry and liver panels are unchanged or show only asymptomatic ALT/AST increases ≤ 2× ULN in 5-10 % of subjects (Watkins et al., 2006, JAMA 296:87-93). No direct CNS markers are altered.  
• Neuro-imaging data (MRI, PET) after therapeutic dosing show no structural changes.  

c) Histopathology  
• No brain or spinal cord lesions were found in rats or dogs in 90-day repeat-dose studies at doses equalling human 4 g day-1 exposure when corrected for body-surface area (ECHA Dossier, 2022).  

Mechanistic interpretation  
• Direct CNS effects are consistent with the pharmacological action (central analgesia via COX-2 inhibition, endocannabinoid enhancement).  
• Mild CNS symptoms (dizziness, somnolence) are likely adaptive pharmacodynamic effects rather than toxic injury.  
• Formation of NAPQI in the brain is measurable but efficiently detoxified by local glutathione at the given dose; therefore oxidative/mitochondrial stress thresholds are not reached.  

Adversity / reversibility / monitorability  
• Adaptive, reversible within hours; easily monitored by routine clinical observation; non-adverse at the MTD under discussion.  

3.2 Peripheral nervous system (PNS)  
• No peripheral neuropathy, altered reflexes or electromyography changes observed in clinical trials or animal studies up to 90 days (Moertel et al., 2008, Drug Saf. 31:85-99).  
• Epidemiology (> 10 000 patient-years) reveals no signal for poly-neuropathy (UK CPRD database analysis, 2019).  
Toxicodynamic activity for the PNS therefore classified as LOW.  

3.3 Sensory systems  
a) Auditory system  
• Several case reports suggested reversible sensorineural hearing loss after very high chronic intake (> 8 g day-1, often with codeine) (Daniel et al., 2013, Otol. Neurotol. 34:1562-1568).  
• No hearing changes detected at 4 g day-1 in placebo-controlled otological assessments (n=120, Smith et al., 2015, Laryngoscope 125:2411-2415).  
Given the higher threshold for this effect, risk at the present MTD is considered VERY LOW.  

b) Visual system  
• No evidence of retinopathy or optic-nerve toxicity in humans or animals.  
• Rare blurred vision events reported (< 0.1 %) but without structural correlate; considered pharmacodynamic, not toxic.  

4. Indirect CNS toxicity secondary to hepatotoxicity  
At doses above the MTD or in individuals with reduced glutathione reserve (e.g., chronic alcohol use, malnutrition) sufficient NAPQI build-up can cause centrilobular hepatic necrosis. If liver failure develops, secondary neurotoxicity can occur:  
• Hepatic encephalopathy (confusion → stupor → coma).  
• Cerebral oedema demonstrated in fulminant hepatic failure (Blei & Cordoba, 2001, J. Hepatol. 34:521-532).  

Mechanism  
• Ammonia accumulation, systemic inflammation, impaired astrocytic glutamate handling, altered blood–brain barrier permeability.  

Relevance to the current 4 g day-1/28-day scenario  
• Probability low in healthy adults but increases with pre-existing risk factors.  
• If it occurs, it is clearly ADVERSE, potentially life-threatening but reversible with N-acetylcysteine and intensive care.  
• Monitorable via serum ammonia, mental-status exam, EEG.  

5. Weight-of-evidence conclusion for 28-day MTD exposure  

Toxicodynamic activity  
• CNS: LOW to MEDIUM (pharmacological CNS activity with minor transient symptoms; no structural toxicity).  
• PNS: LOW.  
• Sensory organs: LOW.  

Systemic availability to nervous tissue: HIGH (drug readily crosses BBB), but detoxification capacity sufficient to prevent structural damage at 4 g day-1.  

Overall, no consistent evidence of direct neurotoxicity is seen at the maximum tolerated therapeutic dose for 28 days in healthy adults. Effects observed are largely adaptive/pharmacodynamic, reversible, and easy to monitor clinically. The principal human safety concern at high repeated doses remains hepatotoxicity, with neurological consequences occurring only secondarily if hepatic failure develops.  

6. Data gaps and uncertainties  
• Limited controlled human data beyond 28 days at 4 g day-1.  
• Sparse histopathology in human brain tissue; reliance on animal data and in vitro studies.  
• Genetic polymorphisms (e.g., CYP2E1, GST) could influence CNS NAPQI production but have not been systematically evaluated.  

7. Ethical and regulatory note  
The assessment aligns with ECHA, FDA and WHO guidelines on repeat-dose toxicity and utilises a weight-of-evidence approach integrating clinical, animal and mechanistic data.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the neurological system (central nervous system, peripheral nervous system and the sensory systems) of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for 28 day where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Metformin or its synonyms with CAS number 657-24-9

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Nervous-system endpoints after 28-day oral administration of a maximum tolerated dose (MTD) of metformin (CAS 657-24-9) to an adult human  

1. Summary assessment  
• Systemic availability: Medium  
  – Oral bioavailability ≈ 50–60 % (Scheen 1996, ECHA)  
  – Rapid absorption (Tmax 2–3 h), no metabolism, renal clearance, steady-state reached in 24–48 h → sustained systemic exposure during the 28-day course.  
• Intrinsic (toxicodynamic) activity toward the nervous system: Low  
  – Metformin is hydrophilic, enters the brain only via organic-cation transporters (OCT2, MATE1); concentration in cerebrospinal fluid is <10 % of plasma (Hundal 2006).  
  – No specific neurotoxic structural alerts; extensive animal repeat-dose studies (≤ 450 mg kg-1 day-1 in rats, 26 wk) show no neuropathology (FDA NDA review, 1994).  
• Weight of evidence (WoE): Clinical experience (>60 years, >120 million patient-years) plus in vivo and in silico data indicate that metformin has a very limited hazard potential for the central, peripheral and sensory nervous systems when used up to the human MTD (≈ 2.5–3 g day-1).  
• Any neurological findings are secondary (e.g. metabolic acidosis) or adaptive (transient taste disturbance).  

2. Observed / predicted neurological findings at the human MTD (28-day exposure)

A. Central nervous system  
1) Headache, dizziness, asthenia  
   – Incidence: 5–9 % in placebo-controlled trials (Glucophage® label, FDA)  
   – Mechanism: Non-specific; possibly mild central serotonergic/adenosinergic modulation or changes in cerebral perfusion linked to gastrointestinal fluid shifts.  
   – Adverse vs adaptive: Mildly adverse (discomfort), not harmful.  
   – Reversibility: Yes, within 24–48 h after discontinuation.  
   – Monitorability: Clinical observation; no special tests required.

2) CNS depression in metformin-associated lactic acidosis (MALA)  
   – Incidence: extremely rare (≈ 3/100 000 patient-years; Lalau 2014).  
   – Functional signs: Confusion, somnolence, agitation, seizures, coma.  
   – Histopathology: None specific; changes are attributable to systemic acidosis/hypoxia rather than direct neuronal injury.  
   – Mechanism: High systemic metformin concentrations (>20 mg L-1) inhibit mitochondrial complex I in liver/kidney → lactate accumulation → systemic pH <7.2 → impaired neuronal ion channel function and cerebral vasodilation.  
   – Adverse: Clearly adverse and life-threatening.  
   – Reversibility: Usually reversible with prompt correction (bicarbonate, hemodialysis).  
   – Monitorability: Serum lactate, blood pH, plasma metformin; neurological status.

B. Peripheral nervous system  
1) Vitamin B12-related peripheral neuropathy  
   – Onset: Typically after ≥6–12 months; not expected in a 28-day study (de Jager 2010).  
   – Mechanism: Metformin interferes with calcium-dependent binding of the IF-B12 complex to ileal cubilin receptors → gradual B12 depletion → demyelination.  
   – Prediction for 28 days: No functional or histological change.  
   – Adverse: Would be adverse if it occurred; reversible with B12 supplementation.  
   – Monitorability: Serum B12, methyl-malonic acid, nerve-conduction studies.

2) Hypoglycaemia-driven neuropathic symptoms (paresthesia)  
   – Metformin alone rarely causes hypoglycaemia; risk only in <1 % (with insufficient caloric intake or concomitant insulin).  
   – Mechanism: Low blood glucose → neuronal glucose deprivation.  
   – Adverse, reversible within minutes to hours after carbohydrate intake; monitorable by capillary glucose.

C. Sensory systems  
1) Dysgeusia (metallic taste)  
   – Incidence: 3–5 %.  
   – Mechanism: Direct interaction with taste-receptor T2Rs or saliva pH alteration; no structural damage.  
   – Adaptive, fully reversible after 1–2 days; monitorable by patient report.

2) Transient visual blurring  
   – Reported mainly during rapid improvement in glycaemia (lens osmolarity shifts).  
   – Mechanism: Adaptive osmotic change, not ocular toxicity.  
   – Reversible within days; monitorable by visual acuity testing.

3) Auditory or vestibular effects  
   – No clinical or preclinical evidence at therapeutic or MTD exposures.

3. Pre-clinical evidence  
• 28-day rat and dog studies up to 900 and 600 mg kg-1 day-1, respectively, showed no microscopic changes in brain, spinal cord, dorsal-root ganglia, peripheral nerves or retina (ECHA dossier; CIIT 1973).  
• No change in motor-activity, grip strength or sensorium in OECD-TG 407-type studies.  
• In vitro neurotoxicity assays (SH-SY5Y, primary cortical neurons) – IC50 >> 10 mM, indicating low intrinsic cytotoxicity (Lake 2017).  

4. Classification of nervous-system hazard for 28-day oral MTD exposure  
• CNS functional effects: Low to medium concern (because of rare MALA).  
• PNS & sensory systems: Low concern.  
Overall toxicodynamic activity: Low  
Systemic availability: Medium  

5. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Lack of dedicated human neuro-behavioural batteries at doses above 3 g day-1.  
• Limited quantitative CSF concentration data at the MTD.  
• Genetic variability in OCT2/MATE1 transporters could modulate brain exposure; data sparse.  
These factors add moderate uncertainty but do not qualitatively change the WoE conclusion.  

6. Conclusions for 28-day MTD scenario  
Metformin exhibits a very low inherent neurotoxic potential. Neurological findings during a 28-day course at the human MTD are expected to be limited to mild, reversible CNS symptoms (headache, dizziness) and taste disturbance. Serious CNS depression can occur only indirectly through the rare event of metformin-associated lactic acidosis; this is clearly adverse but monitorable and reversible with standard medical care. No peripheral neuropathy, structural CNS lesions or sensory-organ toxicity are anticipated within 28 days.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – nervous system effects of orally administering a 28-day “maximum tolerated dose” (MTD) of atorvastatin (CAS 134523-00-5) in an adult human  

1. Context, dose definition and systemic availability  
•  Therapeutic experience: 10–80 mg day-1 for months-years. In Phase III trials 80 mg day-1 was generally the highest dose that could be given without dose-limiting clinical chemistry changes (ALT/AST >3× ULN) – i.e. the de-facto human MTD.  
•  ADME: oral bio-availability ≈ 14 %; extensive first-pass metabolism to active hydroxy-metabolites; 98 % plasma protein bound; log P ≈ 4.2; volume of distribution ≈ 380 L. The parent and metabolites are sufficiently lipophilic to reach the CNS; CSF/ plasma ratios of 0.2–0.3 have been measured (Fong 2016).  
•  Conclusion on systemic availability for the nervous system: HIGH (adequate exposure for a pharmacological or toxicological effect).

2. Weight-of-evidence data set considered  
a.  Pre-registration 4- and 13-week repeat-dose studies in rat and dog (Pfizer NDA 20-702; EMA EPAR).  
b.  Dedicated neurotoxicity screening (FOB, motor activity) in rats up to 120 mg kg-1 day-1 for 4 weeks.  
c.  Clinical trials including TNT (n = 10 001; 80 mg day-1, 5 years), IDEAL, MIRACL, and short-term PK/PD trials of 20–80 mg for 2–8 weeks.  
d.  Epidemiological signal detection databases (FDA FAERS, WHO Vigibase).  
e.  Case series of statin-associated peripheral neuropathy and cognitive effects (e.g. Gaist 2002; Cham et al. 2016; Evans et al. 2019).  
f.  In-silico alert: inhibition of the mevalonate pathway ⇒ depletion of cholesterol, isoprenoids and ubiquinone in neurons.

3. Observed / predicted neuro-toxicological profile at the 28-day MTD  

3.1 Central Nervous System (CNS)  
a. Functional observations  
   – Reversible episodic memory loss, confusion, decreased concentration or “brain-fog”: incidence in short-term RCTs ≤ 0.5 % and usually appearing within the first month (FDA Drug Safety Communication 2012).  
   – Insomnia and vivid dreams reported in ≤ 2 % of subjects (product label).  
   – Headache and dizziness (common class effects, usually mild).  
b. Histopathology / imaging  
   – No statin-related microscopic lesions in brain or spinal cord in rat/dog studies up to 4–6× the human AUC.  
   – MRI or CSF biomarker studies of high-dose atorvastatin (80 mg day-1 for 6 weeks) showed no change in white-matter integrity (Wasser et al. 2016).  
c. Mechanistic explanation  
   – Lipophilic statins can cross the BBB. Inhibition of neuronal HMG-CoA-reductase lowers local cholesterol, essential for synaptic vesicle formation and membrane micro-domains, transiently impairing synaptic transmission and cognitive processing.  
   – Reduced synthesis of isoprenoids (geranylgeranyl-PP, farnesyl-PP) may disturb small-GTPase signalling involved in neurite outgrowth.  
   – Decreased ubiquinone (CoQ10) compromises mitochondrial ATP production, producing reversible neuronal energy deficit.  
d. Adversity, reversibility, monitoring  
   •  Adverse? YES when cognitive disturbance affects daily living; otherwise adaptive/non-adverse.  
   •  Reversible? YES (median recovery 2–4 weeks post-discontinuation).  
   •  Monitorable? Mini-Cog, MoCA, patient-reported outcome questionnaires.

3.2 Peripheral Nervous System (PNS)  
a. Functional observations  
   – Very rare sensory-predominant axonal neuropathy (estimated reporting rate 1.3–4 cases /100 000 patient-years; Gaist 2002). Presents after ≥ 1 month but can arise earlier at high doses. Symptoms: distal paresthesia, numbness, reduced vibratory sense.  
   – Myasthenia-like syndrome and ocular/bulbar weakness (dozens of case reports, class effect).  
b. Nerve-conduction / histology  
   – Electrophysiology: reduced sensory nerve action potential amplitude; mild slowing of conduction velocity.  
   – Sural nerve biopsy (sporadic cases) shows length-dependent axonal degeneration without demyelination or inflammation.  
c. Mechanistic explanation  
   – Same mevalonate-pathway inhibition impairs cholesterol supply for Schwann-cell membranes and myelin maintenance.  
   – Depletion of geranylgeranylated proteins interferes with axonal cytoskeleton dynamics and fast axonal transport.  
   – Mitochondrial dysfunction via CoQ10 lowering increases oxidative stress in long peripheral axons.  
d. Adversity, reversibility, monitoring  
   •  Adverse? YES (sensory loss is clinically significant).  
   •  Reversible? Partly; improvement typically seen within 3–6 months after withdrawal, but full recovery not universal.  
   •  Monitorable? Neurological exam, quantitative sensory testing, nerve-conduction studies, serum CK (to exclude myopathy overlap).

3.3 Sensory systems  
a. Vision: blurred vision, diplopia and very rare optic neuritis / ocular myasthenia. Generally reversible.  
b. Hearing: sporadic tinnitus cases; causal link uncertain.  
Mechanism: thought secondary to cranial neuropathy or neuromuscular junction impairment.

4. Clinical pathology correlates  
•  There are no neuro-specific serum biomarkers.  
•  CK elevation reflects muscle, not nerve injury, but is useful differential.  
•  Low plasma/CSF CoQ10 has been documented in statin users (Caso 2007) but remains a research tool.

5. Overall classification  
Toxicodynamic activity toward the nervous system: LOW-to-MEDIUM. The effects are infrequent, mostly functional, without overt structural damage at 28 days.  
Systemic availability: HIGH – atorvastatin and active metabolites reach the CNS/PNS at pharmacologically relevant concentrations.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps  
•  Controlled trials were not specifically powered for neurotoxicity endpoints; incidence estimates derive mainly from spontaneous reporting.  
•  Genetic susceptibility (SLCO1B1, CYP3A4) may modulate exposure and toxicity but is poorly quantified for neuro-effects.  
•  Histopathology data from humans are limited to case biopsies; long-term low-grade degeneration cannot be excluded.

7. Conclusion  
At 28 days of daily oral administration at the human MTD (80 mg), atorvastatin has a low-frequency but plausible potential to cause reversible, predominantly functional disturbances of the CNS (mild cognitive effects, sleep disturbance) and PNS (length-dependent sensory neuropathy, ocular/bulbar weakness). These effects are mechanistically linked to inhibition of neuronal cholesterol and isoprenoid biosynthesis and ensuing mitochondrial dysfunction. They are monitorable in clinical practice and usually reversible after dose reduction or discontinuation, although persistent neuropathy can occur in rare cases.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Prednisone (CAS 53-03-2)  
Synonyms: Deltacortril, Meticorten, 17,21-dihydroxypregna-1,4-diene-3,11,20-trione  

Scenario evaluated  
• Species: Adult human  
• Regimen: Oral administration once daily for 28 days at the maximum tolerated dose (clinical MTD ≈ 0.8–1 mg kg-1 day-1, i.e. 60–80 mg day-1 for a 70-kg adult; Kanda et al., 2015; FDA clinical pharmacology reviews).  
• End points of interest: Functional and structural effects on the central nervous system (CNS), peripheral nervous system (PNS) and sensory systems.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Systemic availability and toxicodynamic activity
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Absorption: Rapid and complete oral absorption (F ≈ 0.8–0.95).  
• Distribution: Prednisone is reduced in the liver to the active metabolite prednisolone, 70–90 % plasma-protein bound; log P 1.5; VD 0.4–1.0 L kg-1. Prednisolone freely crosses the blood-brain barrier (BBB) through carrier-mediated diffusion (Edelson et al., 2011).  
• Metabolism / Excretion: Hepatic 11-β-HSD-I reduction, CYP3A-mediated hydroxylations, conjugation → urinary excretion of glucuronides. t½ prednisolone 2.5–3.5 h.  
• Resulting systemic availability for the nervous system: HIGH.  
• Primary molecular targets: Cytosolic glucocorticoid receptor (GR, NR3C1) → genomic and non-genomic signalling; some affinity for mineralocorticoid receptor (MR).  
Toxicodynamic activity on nervous tissue: MEDIUM to HIGH (potent receptor agonism, but no direct cytotoxicity).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Observed / predicted neurological effects at MTD
--------------------------------------------------------------------
A. Central nervous system
--------------------------------
1. Mood and behavioural changes  
   – Incidence: 5–20 % at ≥ 40 mg day-1 for ≥ 1 week (Brown & Chandler, 2001; Ciriaco et al., 2013).  
   – Phenotypes: Euphoria, irritability, anxiety, insomnia, agitation; less frequently depression or dysphoria.  
   – Mechanism: Rapid non-genomic GR activation alters monoaminergic and glutamatergic transmission (increase in dopamine release, decreased serotonin turnover). Genomic effects down-regulate brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) and inhibit hippocampal neurogenesis (Sapolsky et al., 2000).  
   – Histopathology / imaging: In animal studies, chronic high-dose prednisolone (30 mg kg-1 day-1) for 4 weeks reduced dentate gyrus neurogenesis and caused mild CA3 dendritic atrophy (Herman et al., 2012). Analogous MRI hippocampal volume loss has been reported in long-term human users (> 3 months); for 28 days, changes are functional, not structural.  
   – Adverse? Yes, when causing clinically relevant psychiatric disturbance.  
   – Reversible? Largely reversible within 1–4 weeks after tapering (Wada et al., 2001).  
   – Monitorable: Neuropsychiatric inventory, sleep diaries, patient-reported outcomes.

2. Acute steroid psychosis / mania  
   – Incidence: 1–2 % at doses ≥ 80 mg day-1 prednisolone equivalents; onset usually within 5–15 days (Lewis & Smith, 1983).  
   – Manifestations: Mania, hallucinations, disorganised thinking.  
   – Mechanism: GR-mediated dysregulation of limbic circuit excitability; elevated cortisol levels enhance glutamate and suppress GABA tone in prefrontal cortex.  
   – Adverse? Clearly adverse.  
   – Reversible? Generally reversible within days–weeks after dose reduction ± antipsychotics.  
   – Monitorable: Mental-status examination; serum drug concentrations not predictive.

3. Cognitive impairment  
   – Observation: Transient deficits in working memory and declarative memory reported in 10–15 % of patients on ≥ 60 mg day-1 for ≥ 2 weeks (Keane et al., 1988).  
   – Mechanism: Hippocampal GR saturation → suppression of long-term potentiation, decreased BDNF.  
   – Adverse? Usually mild and reversible; considered adaptive if transient, adverse if it affects daily living.  
   – Reversible/monitorable: Yes / neuropsychological testing.

4. Seizures (very rare)  
   – Case reports in patients receiving pulse doses (> 1 g methylprednisolone); not expected at 1 mg kg-1 day-1.  
   – Mechanism: Electrolyte disturbances (hypocalcaemia, hyponatraemia) or GR-mediated lowering of seizure threshold.  
   – Relevance to current scenario: Unlikely but cannot be excluded in predisposed individuals.  
   – Monitorable: EEG in high-risk patients.

B. Peripheral nervous system
--------------------------------
1. Steroid-induced myopathy (proximal muscle weakness rather than nerve lesion)  
   – Incidence: 2–10 % after ≥ 4 weeks high-dose; begins with lower-limb weakness.  
   – Mechanism: GR-driven up-regulation of ubiquitin–proteasome pathway, inhibition of IGF-1 signalling in skeletal muscle.  
   – Neuropathy per se: No convincing evidence of direct axonal or demyelinating peripheral neuropathy within 28 days.  
   – Adverse? Myopathy is adverse; onset may start at 28 days.  
   – Reversible? Partially, recovery over weeks–months after discontinuation.  
   – Monitorable: Manual muscle testing, serum CK (often normal), EMG.

2. Electrolyte-mediated neuro-muscular irritability  
   – Prednisone has weak mineralocorticoid activity → kaliuresis → hypokalaemia in susceptible individuals, provoking paraesthesia or muscle cramps.  
   – Adverse? Usually mild; considered adaptive until clinically significant.  
   – Reversible / monitorable: Yes; serum electrolytes.

C. Sensory systems
-------------------------
1. Vision  
   – Short-term: Transient blurred vision due to fluid shifts; usually non-pathogenic.  
   – Long-term (> months): Posterior sub-capsular cataracts, ocular hypertension. Not anticipated within 28 days although IOP rise can occur early in steroid responders.  
   – Mechanism: GR-mediated changes in lens epithelial cell differentiation; increased Na⁺/Cl⁻ transport in trabecular meshwork.  
   – Adverse? Blurred vision at 28 days is generally adaptive/reversible; IOP rise (if present) is adverse.  
   – Monitorable: Ophthalmologic exam, tonometry.

2. Hearing and vestibular function  
   – No consistent evidence of ototoxicity at therapeutic or high oral doses.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Clinical pathology correlates
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Serum cortisol: suppressed to < 50 nmol L-1 after 2–3 days → indicates complete HPA suppression (expected, adaptive).  
• Electrolytes: mild hypokalaemia, hypernatremia possible (mechanistically linked to neuro-muscular irritability).  
• No specific neurochemical biomarkers are routinely available; neuroimaging or CSF analysis is reserved for research settings.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Weight-of-evidence statement
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The 28-day high-dose scenario produces HIGH systemic availability in brain and peripheral tissues. The dominant toxicodynamic mode of action is pharmacological GR agonism. The CNS is the most sensitive nervous compartment; functional (rather than structural) neuro-psychiatric disturbances are common, mechanistically linked to acute and sub-acute GR-mediated modulation of neurotransmission and neurotrophic support. Overt neuropathology (neuronal loss, demyelination) is not expected within 28 days in humans, though animal data show hippocampal dendritic retraction at comparable exposure.

Peripheral nerve involvement is minimal; muscle and electrolyte changes account for most peripheral signs. Sensory effects are limited to reversible visual disturbances; clinically significant ocular pathology usually requires longer exposure.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Summary table of key effects

Effect | Nervous compartment | MOA | Adverse vs adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
------ | ------------------- | --- | ------------------- | ----------- | -----------------------
Mood/behaviour changes | CNS | GR modulation of monoamines | Adverse if symptomatic | Yes (days–weeks) | Psychiatric assessment
Steroid psychosis/mania | CNS | Limbic GR overshoot | Adverse | Yes | Mental-status exam
Cognitive impairment | CNS | Hippocampal GR, ↓BDNF | Mild adaptive→adverse | Yes | Neurocognitive tests
Seizures (rare) | CNS | Electrolyte shift, lowered threshold | Adverse | Yes | EEG, electrolytes
Steroid myopathy | PNS (muscle) | Proteasome activation | Adverse | Partial | Strength testing, EMG
Hypokalaemic paraesthesia | PNS | Mineralocorticoid activity | Adaptive→adverse | Yes | Serum K⁺
Blurred vision / ↑IOP | Sensory (eye) | Fluid shifts / trabecular GR | Adaptive (blur) / Adverse (IOP↑) | Yes | Ophthalmology

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Data gaps and uncertainties
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• No controlled human histopathology for 28-day exposure.  
• Limited quantitative dose-response data for neuropsychiatric endpoints at the upper MTD in healthy adults (most data from patients with underlying disease).  
• Inter-individual variability due to GR polymorphisms and CYP3A5 expression not fully characterised.  
• Animal studies often use higher mg kg-1 doses than clinical MTD; extrapolation introduces uncertainty.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Overall conclusion
Prednisone given orally at the human MTD for 28 days is not a frank neurotoxicant in the classical sense (no direct neuron- or glia-specific cytotoxicity), but its robust pharmacological action on glucocorticoid receptors leads to a HIGH likelihood of functional CNS disturbances (mood, behaviour, cognition) and a MEDIUM likelihood of peripheral neuromuscular effects. These effects are considered ADVERSE when they impair daily functioning. Most manifestations are reversible and clinically monitorable, supporting risk management through dose titration, psychiatric vigilance, electrolyte monitoring, and ophthalmologic surveillance.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: 2,3,7,8-Tetrachloro-p-dibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, “dioxin”)  
CAS No.: 1746-01-6  
Scenario modelled: Repeated oral administration of a Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD) to an adult human, once daily for 28 days. Focus: neuro-toxicity (CNS, PNS, sensory systems).

Note on the MTD
•  In sub-chronic rodent studies an MTD of 0.1–1 µg kg-1 day-1 (rats) or 0.025–0.05 µg kg-1 day-1 (mice) produces pronounced systemic toxicity (wasting syndrome, hepatic enzyme induction, immunotoxicity) but ≥90 % survival (Kociba 1978; NTP 2006).  
•  In primates a no-observable-adverse-effect level (NOAEL) of ≈0.001 µg kg-1 day-1 has been derived for chronic intake; overt toxicity occurs ≥0.01 µg kg-1 day-1 (Allen 1977).  
Extrapolating allometricly and applying standard MTD criteria (≤10 % body-weight loss, no lethality) gives a human MTD in the range 0.005–0.02 µg kg-1 day-1 (0.35–1.4 µg day-1 for a 70 kg adult). This dose would raise serum TEQ levels to >10 000 pg g-1 lipid within 3–4 weeks—orders of magnitude above current background (≈3–5 pg g-1)—and clearly into a toxic range (EPA 2012).

Systemic availability (ADME)  
•  Absorption: 40–60 % of an oral dose is absorbed in humans and non-human primates (Poiger & Schlatter 1986) → MEDIUM-to-HIGH.  
•  Distribution: Highly lipophilic (logKow ≈ 7); distributes to adipose tissue, liver, and readily crosses the blood–brain barrier → HIGH.  
•  Metabolism: Very slow oxidative metabolism via CYP1A2; strong auto-induction.  
•  Elimination: Half-life in humans 7–11 years; faecal/biliary excretion of conjugates.  
Overall systemic availability for the nervous system: HIGH (steady accumulation in lipid-rich neural tissue during the 28-day period).

Observed / predicted neurological effects under the 28-day MTD scenario  
(The table groups the weight-of-evidence from adult animal studies, accidental human poisonings, and mechanistic data. Developmental neurotoxicity, though well documented, is not included because the subject is an adult.)

1. Central Nervous System (CNS)
   a. Functional observations  
      – Decreased spontaneous locomotor activity, hypo-reactivity, lethargy (rat and mouse sub-chronic studies at ≥0.1 µg kg-1 day-1; Murray 1979).  
      – Impaired sensorimotor coordination (rotarod, grip strength) in rats after 30 days at 0.3 µg kg-1 day-1 (Seo et al. 2008).  
      – EEG alterations (increased β-activity, decreased α-activity) in rhesus monkeys given 0.02 µg kg-1 day-1 for 20 days (Allen 1977).  
      – Neuropsychological changes (headache, irritability, short-term memory deficits) reported in adult workers and Yusho/Yucheng cohorts with serum TCDD ≥ 2 000 pg g-1 lipid (Uemura 2009).  
   b. Histopathology / clinical pathology  
      – No overt neuronal necrosis.  
      – Astrocyte hypertrophy (GFAP ↑) in cerebral cortex and hippocampus of rats (Klinefelter 2019).  
      – Microglial activation and elevated brain pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α).  
      – Brain weight unchanged; mild cerebellar Purkinje cell shrinkage described in mice.  
   c. Mechanistic interpretation  
      – High-affinity activation of the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) in neurons/glia → transcription of CYP1A1/1B1 and redox cycling → ROS production, mitochondrial stress.  
      – AhR-NF-κB crosstalk → neuro-inflammation.  
      – Secondary effects from systemic wasting (hypoglycaemia, altered thyroid hormones) aggravate CNS functional decline.  
   d. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
      • Adversity: Decreased locomotor activity and cognitive changes are ADVERSE.  
      • Reversibility: Partial; functional deficits improve once body burden falls, but given the multi-year half-life full reversal is uncertain.  
      • Clinical monitoring: Neurobehavioural tests, EEG, MRI spectroscopy (neuro-inflammation markers).

2. Peripheral Nervous System (PNS)
   a. Functional observations  
      – Slowed nerve conduction velocity (NCV) in sciatic/tibial nerves of rats at 0.1 µg kg-1 day-1 for 90 days (Peters et al. 1999); similar findings in Yucheng men 15 years post-exposure.  
      – Decreased compound muscle action potential amplitude, mild distal weakness.  
   b. Histopathology  
      – Mild segmental demyelination and focal axonal degeneration in peripheral nerves of guinea pigs and rats (Goldstein et al. 1977).  
   c. Mechanistic interpretation  
      – TCDD-induced CYP1A/ROS in Schwann cells → lipid peroxidation of myelin; AhR mediated suppression of neuregulin/ErbB signalling needed for myelin maintenance.  
      – Indirect contribution from systemic protein-calorie deficit (“wasting”).  
   d. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
      • Adverse.  
      • Largely reversible over months to years if exposure ceases; remyelination documented in animals.  
      • Clinical tools: NCV, EMG, quantitative sensory testing, serum neurofilament light chain.

3. Sensory systems
   a. Auditory  
      – Adult data scant; no consistent ototoxicity in short-term exposure. Developmental exposures cause high-frequency hearing loss (Lu et al. 2013) but not expected in adults after 28 days.  
   b. Visual  
      – Transient colour vision deficiency reported in industrial cohort with acute high exposure (Nishijo 2014); mechanism not resolved.  
   c. Olfactory / gustatory  
      – No clear-cut data; in animals, olfactory bulb CYP1A1 induction occurs but without functional loss.  
   Conclusions for sensory organs: At the adult 28-day MTD, no predictable frank structural lesion; any functional disturbances would be mild, adaptive or secondary to CNS/PNS effects, and expected to be reversible.

Overall weight-of-evidence statement
• Toxicodynamic activity toward nervous tissue at the 28-day MTD: MEDIUM-to-HIGH.  
• Systemic availability for target tissues: HIGH.  
• Primary mode of action: High-affinity AhR activation → dysregulated transcription, oxidative stress, metabolic/immune secondary cascades.  
• The nervous system is not the most sensitive adult target (liver, immune and endocrine are), but measurable neuro-functional changes and peripheral nerve pathology are plausible and supported by animal and limited human evidence.  
• Effects are adverse, partly reversible, and clinically monitorable, though recovery is protracted due to the long biological half-life of TCDD.

Uncertainties and data gaps
1. Human adult data from controlled exposures do not exist; conclusions rely on accidental poisonings and occupational cohorts where co-exposure to PCBs/PCDFs occurred.  
2. Rodent MTDs may not faithfully predict human threshold for CNS/PNS toxicity because wasting limits dose escalation in animals but not necessarily in humans with supportive care.  
3. Quantitative dose-effect relationships for specific neuro-end-points (e.g., cognitive tests) are insufficient; benchmark doses could not be derived.  
4. Inter-individual susceptibility factors (AhR polymorphisms, nutritional status) add variability not captured here.

Key references
•  Kociba RJ et al. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 1978;44:1-14.  
•  Murray FJ et al. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 1979;50:467-478.  
•  Allen JR et al. Toxicol Appl Pharmacol 1977;41:279-320.  
•  EPA. Reanalysis of Key Issues Related to Dioxin Toxicity and Response to NAS Comments. 2012.  
•  Peters JM et al. Toxicol Sci 1999;50:79-87.  
•  Uemura H et al. Occup Environ Med 2009;66:614-618.  
•  Seo JW et al. J Appl Toxicol 2008;28:147-155.  
•  Klinefelter GR et al. Neurotoxicology 2019;74:319-329.

This assessment follows a weight-of-evidence approach, integrates experimental and epidemiological findings, and recognises remaining uncertainties.

tests.prompt: Describe the known or predicted toxicological effects (if any) on the neurological system (central nervous system, peripheral nervous system and the sensory systems) of administering a maximum tolerated dose of {chemical_and_CASRN} to an adult human for 28 day where the dose is repeated each day by the oral route. Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. For any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. For all toxicological effects observed determine if they are adverse or adaptive, reversible and monitorable in the clinic.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested: 28-day, once-daily oral administration of benzene (CAS 71-43-2; synonyms: benzol, phenyl hydride) to an adult human at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD).  Focus is on neurological (central, peripheral and sensory) endpoints.

1. Physico-chemical properties relevant for nervous-system exposure
• MW 78.11; logKow 2.13; vp 12.7 kPa (25 °C)  
• Highly lipophilic enough to traverse biological membranes, yet sufficiently water-soluble (~1.8 g L-1) to circulate systemically.

2. ADME and systemic availability
Absorption: ≥ 90 % absorbed from the human GI tract (ATSDR, 2022).  
Distribution: Rapidly enters richly perfused organs; brain:blood ratios 3- to 5-fold within 30-60 min in rodents (Ghantous 2015).  
Metabolism: Phase-I oxidation (CYP2E1 > CYP2B6, 1A2) → benzene oxide ⇆ phenol → catechol, hydroquinone, benzoquinones; minor ring-opening to trans,trans-muconaldehyde. Phase-II conjugation (SULT, UGT).  
Excretion: t½blood ≈ 5-7 h; metabolites in urine within 24 h.  
Systemic availability rating: HIGH (rapid, extensive absorption and BBB penetration).

3. Weight-of-evidence for neurological effects after repeated exposure
Primary information sources: ATSDR (2022); ECHA dossier (2018); EPA IRIS (2002); IARC Monograph 120 (2018); animal oral studies (Dawson et al., 2010; Wolf et al., 1956); occupational inhalation studies (Kuang et al., 2007; Zupanic et al., 2019); case reports of acute intoxication (Rothman 1997).

3.1 Functional CNS effects
Observed / predicted:
a) Transient CNS depression: headache, dizziness, euphoria, ataxia, drowsiness within 0.5–1 h of each dose—classic “solvent narcosis”.  
b) Sub-acute psychomotor slowing and reduced short-term memory after 3-4 weeks at high intakes (extrapolated from workers exposed to ≥ 50 ppm inhalation; Anger et al., 1986).  
c) Electrophysiology: mild slowing of EEG α-rhythm reported in shoe-factory workers (Kuang et al., 2007).

Histopathology: No reproducible neuronal necrosis at sub-lethal oral doses. In mice gavaged 800 mg kg-1 d-1 for 14 d, slight vacuolation in cerebellar Purkinje cells was described (Wolf 1956); not confirmed by later studies.  

Clinical pathology: Occasional elevation of serum prolactin (dopaminergic disruption) in chronically exposed workers (Chen et al., 2012).

Mechanism:
• Narcosis—non-specific disruption of lipid bilayer fluidity; potentiation of GABA_A activity, inhibition of NMDA currents (Wallace 2003).  
• Cognitive changes—oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction from phenolic metabolites in the CNS; hydroquinone generates ROS, reduces glutathione.  
Adversity assessment: CNS depression – Adverse (impairs function) but Acutely Reversible; monitor by neurobehavioural test battery, psychomotor vigilance, EEG.

3.2 Peripheral nervous system (PNS)
Evidence:
• Case reports of peripheral neuropathy after accidental ingestion of 150–200 mL benzene for 5–7 days (Yu et al., 2002); nerve-conduction velocity ↓ 15–20 %.  
• 90-day rat oral study (NTP, 1986) at 200 mg kg-1 d-1: no axonal degeneration in sciatic nerve.  
Weight-of-evidence: Very limited; threshold appears above the haemato-toxicity MTD.  
Mechanism: Axonal oxidative injury by quinone metabolites; protein adduct formation with tubulin.  
Adversity: Would be Adverse if it occurs; potentially Reversible if myelin intact; monitorable via EMG/NCV.

Toxicodynamic activity rating for PNS: LOW to MEDIUM (data sparse, effects only at very high doses).

3.3 Sensory systems
• Vision: Workers exposed to ≥ 100 ppm complain of blurred vision, but objective ophthalmic deficits not confirmed (Yin 2011).  
• Auditory: No consistent audiometric shifts in cohorts; one rat inhalation study suggested outer-hair-cell loss at 300 ppm for 13 wk, but not reproduced.  
• Olfactory/Vestibular: Acute vertigo secondary to CNS depression.

Overall sensory toxicodynamic activity: LOW.

4. Integrated adverse-outcome pathway (AOP) perspective
Molecular initiating event: CYP2E1-mediated epoxidation → benzene oxide.  
Key events relevant to nervous tissue:
1. Generation of hydroquinone/benzoquinone → ROS, quinone-protein adducts.  
2. Oxidative damage to neuronal mitochondria → ATP depletion.  
3. Neurotransmission modulation (GABA_A potentiation, NMDA inhibition).  
4. Functional outcomes: acute narcosis, cognitive slowing, possible peripheral neuropathy.

5. Summary of expected findings at the human oral MTD for 28 days
CNS:
• Daily transient solvent narcosis (dizziness, ataxia, somnolence).  
• Mild, sub-clinical changes in reaction time or short-term memory after ≥ 2 weeks (detectable by neuropsychological testing).  
PNS:
• Unlikely at the MTD but possible mild sensory neuropathy if dose pushes above haemato-toxicity threshold.  
Sensory organs:
• No specific histopathology; complaints of blurred vision/vertigo are functional and reversible.

All endpoints lie well below the more sensitive haematopoietic toxicity; therefore, neurological toxicity would seldom be dose-limiting in humans, yet remains clinically relevant for safety.

6. Clinical monitoring / reversibility
• Acute signs: observation for somnolence, finger-to-nose test, Romberg, Mini-Mental State every few days.  
• Laboratory: serum prolactin, oxidative-stress markers (8-OH-dG in urine) — investigational.  
• Neurophysiology: baseline and day-28 EEG, NCV if symptoms arise.  
Reversibility: High for narcosis and cognitive slowing (hours-days after exposure cessation); peripheral neuropathy, if any, likely reversible over weeks-months provided axon integrity retained.

7. Uncertainties and data gaps
• Human oral data are scarce; inhalation data extrapolated assuming equivalent internal dose (Pbpk models suggest comparable AUCblood).  
• Histopathology in animal oral studies limited in scope and sensitivity; modern stereology or TEM not applied.  
• Possible interactive effects with ethanol (competitive CYP2E1 substrate) not addressed.

8. Overall ratings
Systemic availability: HIGH  
Toxicodynamic activity toward nervous system: MEDIUM (CNS) / LOW–MEDIUM (PNS & sensory)  
Confidence: Moderate (robust evidence for acute CNS effects; limited for chronic neurotoxicity after 28-day oral exposure).

Key references
ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Benzene. 2022.  
ECHA. Benzene – Registration Dossier. 2018.  
EPA IRIS. Benzene (CASRN 71-43-2). 2002.  
IARC. Benzene. Monograph 120. 2018.  
Anger WK et al. Neurobehavioral evaluation of workers exposed to benzene. Neurotoxicology 1986.  
Wolf MA et al. Toxicological studies on benzene. AMA Arch Ind Health 1956.  
Kuang D et al. EEG changes in benzene-exposed workers. J Occup Environ Med 2007.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: “Arsenite” – trivalent inorganic arsenic ion, represented in practice by sodium arsenite (NaAsO2, CAS 15502-74-6) or potassium arsenite.  
Scenario: Daily oral administration of the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) to an adult human for 28 consecutive days.  
Focus: Functional and morphologic effects on  
• Central nervous system (CNS)  
• Peripheral nervous system (PNS)  
• Sensory systems (visual, auditory, olfactory, gustatory, somatosensory)  

Summary conclusion (weight of evidence)  
1. Systemic availability: HIGH – 60–95 % oral absorption; extensive systemic distribution; readily crosses the blood–brain barrier (BBB) and the blood–nerve barrier.  
2. Toxicodynamic activity toward nervous tissue: HIGH – potent multisite electrophile that binds sulfhydryl groups, inhibits mitochondrial enzymes, and induces oxidative stress.  
3. Principal 28-day effects at an MTD:  
   • Sensory-predominant, length-dependent axonal neuropathy (numbness, paraesthesia, pain, reduced vibration sense, slowed nerve-conduction velocity).  
   • Early functional CNS changes (headache, fatigue, irritability, memory impairment, slowed cognitive processing, mild tremor).  
   • Subclinical visual and auditory sensory deficits (contrast-sensitivity loss, reduced brain-stem auditory evoked potentials).  
   • Histopathology: distal axon degeneration ± segmental demyelination in peripheral nerves; neuronal loss and gliosis in hippocampus/cerebellum; swollen mitochondria; increased brain lipid peroxidation.  
4. All identified effects are adverse. Most peripheral effects are partially reversible; central effects show only limited reversibility. All can be monitored clinically (neuro-physiology, neuro-imaging, neuro-psychology, body-fluid speciation).  

1. Absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion (ADME) – determinants of systemic availability  
• Absorption: Inorganic As(III) is rapidly and almost completely absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract (ATSDR, 2007; ECHA dossier).  
• Distribution: Reversible binding to hemoglobin and plasma proteins; penetrates BBB by aquaglyceroporins (AQP7, AQP9). Peak brain concentrations occur within 2–4 h in rodents (Vahter, 2002).  
• Metabolism: Hepatic oxidative methylation → monomethylarsonous acid (MMAIII), monomethylarsonic acid (MMAV), dimethylarsinous acid (DMAIII), dimethylarsinic acid (DMAV). As(III) and MMAIII are the most neurotoxic species.  
• Excretion: Predominantly renal (half-life ~10 h for inorganic As, 20–30 h for methylated species).  
• Bioaccumulation in nervous tissue is limited but continuous exposure maintains a steady-state pool.  

2. Functional neuro-toxicity after 28-day MTD exposure  

2.1 Peripheral nervous system  
Observed / predicted effects  
• Early: burning sensation of palms/soles, distal paraesthesia, hyperaesthesia.  
• By week 3–4: sensory loss in “stocking-glove” distribution, reduced deep-tendon reflexes, impaired vibration sense, distal muscle weakness.  
• Nerve-conduction studies: 15-40 % slowing of sensory and motor conduction velocity, reduced action-potential amplitude (Norboo et al., 1994; Roels et al., 2020).  

Histopathology (animal & occasional human biopsies)  
• Distal axonopathy with Wallerian degeneration; secondary segmental demyelination.  
• Schwann-cell proliferation and onion-bulb formation in chronic cases.  
• Mild chromatolysis of dorsal-root-ganglion neurons (Vahidnia et al., 2007; Zhang et al., 2021).  

Mechanistic explanation  
• As(III) binds lipoic-acid–dependent E3 subunit of pyruvate dehydrogenase complex → ATP depletion in long axons.  
• Reacts with cysteine residues of antioxidant enzymes (glutathione reductase, thioredoxin reductase) → ↑ROS → lipid peroxidation of axolemma.  
• Disrupts axonal transport by inhibiting kinesin and dynein ATPases (Mishra et al., 2014).  
Outcome classification  
• Adverse, partly reversible (remyelination, axonal regrowth requires months).  
• Clinically monitorable: Quantitative sensory testing, nerve-conduction studies, serum neuron-specific enolase (NSE).  

2.2 Central nervous system  
Functional effects  
• Non-specific: headache, dizziness, irritability, sleep disturbance (case reports of medicinal Fowler’s solution; ATSDR, 2007).  
• Cognitive: decreased working memory, processing speed (cross-sectional data from endemic areas, e.g., Wasserman et al., 2004; animal Morris-water-maze deficits after 2–4 week arsenite).  
• Motor: mild postural tremor, slowed finger-tapping.  

Histopathology / neurochemistry (rodents exposed 10–50 ppm As(III) in drinking water for 4 weeks)  
• Neuronal loss in CA1, dentate gyrus; Purkinje-cell loss in cerebellum.  
• Astro- and micro-gliosis; increased 8-hydroxy-2´-deoxyguanosine (8-OHdG); reduced brain glutathione.  
• Decreased synaptophysin and PSD-95 (synaptic density markers).  

Mechanistic explanation  
• Oxidative stress–driven mitochondrial permeability-transition opening → apoptosis.  
• Inhibition of acetylcholinesterase and altered dopaminergic/glutamatergic signalling.  
• Epigenetic modulation (DNA & histone hypomethylation) affecting neuro-developmental genes (i.e., BDNF).  

Outcome classification  
• Adverse. Reversibility limited – some functional recovery possible with nerve-growth-factor induction but neuronal loss is permanent.  
• Monitorable: neuro-psychological batteries, MRI (hippocampal volume), proton MRS for NAA/Cr ratio, blood/urine arsenic speciation.  

2.3 Sensory systems  

A. Vision  
• Functional: Decreased contrast sensitivity, delayed visual evoked potentials (VEPs) (Mousa & Monsef, 2014).  
• Histology (rats): loss of photoreceptor outer segments, RPE vacuolation, retinal ganglion-cell apoptosis.  
Mechanism: Vascular endothelial damage + ROS-mediated photoreceptor apoptosis.  
Reversibility: Partial; RPE injury may be permanent.  

B. Audition  
• Brain-stem auditory evoked response (BAER) latency prolongation in 4-week As-treated guinea pigs; mild high-frequency hearing loss in chronically exposed workers (Prasad et al., 2011).  
Mechanism: As-induced oxidative injury to outer-hair-cell mitochondria; interference with KCNQ4 channels.  
Reversibility: Possible if hair-cell loss is not complete.  

C. Olfaction/Gustation  
• Metallic taste, transient hyposmia reported during therapeutic arsenical treatment (1940s literature). Mechanism: Direct interaction with taste-receptor thiols; generally reversible within days after cessation.  

3. Clinical pathology and biomarkers  
• Increased plasma/CSF protein carbonyls, MDA, 8-OHdG.  
• Elevated urinary AsIII + MMAIII proportion relative to total arsenic reflects incomplete methylation at high doses.  
• Slightly elevated serum NSE and S100β correlate with neuronal injury.  

4. Adversity, adaptation, reversibility, clinical monitoring  

Effect | Adverse vs adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable?  
Peripheral sensory neuropathy | Adverse | Partially (months) | NCS, QST, skin biopsy (IENF density)  
CNS cognitive slowing | Adverse | Limited | Neuro-psych tests, MRI/MRS  
Headache, fatigue | Adverse, nonspecific | Rapid | Symptom reporting  
Visual/auditory deficits | Adverse | Partial | VEP/ERG, audiometry  
Oxidative-stress biomarker changes | Adaptive at low level; becomes adverse when overwhelming | Yes upon cessation | Blood 8-OHdG, GSH/GSSG  

5. Data gaps & uncertainty  
• No controlled 28-day human MTD study exists; conclusions rely on accidental, medicinal and environmental exposures plus sub-chronic animal studies (rat, mouse, guinea-pig).  
• Extrapolation across species assumes similar toxicodynamics; human peripheral nerves are longer and may be more vulnerable.  
• Individual variability in arsenic methylation (AS3MT polymorphisms) introduces uncertainty in hazard magnitude.  

Key references (representative)  
• ATSDR. Toxicological Profile for Arsenic. Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry, 2007.  
• IARC Monograph 100C, 2012.  
• Vahidnia, A. et al. Effects of arsenic on the nervous system. Hum Exp Toxicol 2007.  
• Wasserman, G.A. et al. Water arsenic exposure and children’s intellectual function. Environ Health Perspect 2004.  
• Zhang, J. et al. Arsenic-induced peripheral neuropathy in mice. Arch Toxicol 2021.  
• Roels, J. et al. Peripheral nerve function in arsenic-exposed workers. Occup Environ Med 2020.  

Overall expert judgment  
Continuous oral exposure to an MTD of arsenite for 28 days is expected to produce clear, clinically detectable neurotoxicity, dominated by a sensory axonal neuropathy and supplemented by milder but still adverse CNS and special-sense deficits. While some functional recovery can occur after exposure ceases, complete reversibility—especially for central neuronal loss—is unlikely. Routine clinical neurology tools combined with arsenic speciation in urine and blood provide adequate monitoring capability.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) of methylmercury (CAS 16056-34-1) to an adult human once daily for 28 days, with a focus on the nervous system.

1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Absorption: ≈ 90–95 % of an oral dose is absorbed in the small intestine (ATSDR, 2022).  
• Distribution: Circulates as the methylmercury-cysteine complex and is taken up into the brain by the large neutral amino-acid transporter LAT1, freely crosses the blood–brain and placental barriers. Brain/blood ratio ≈ 5–10 after steady state (Clarkson & Magos, 2006).  
• Metabolism / Excretion: Slow demethylation by intestinal microflora to inorganic Hg. Whole-body t½ ≈ 50 days; apparent brain t½ longer.  
Systemic availability: HIGH.

2. Reference point for the “maximum tolerated dose”  
No deliberate human dosing data exist. 20–50 µg/kg bw day⁻¹ for 1–2 months already produced clear neuro-symptoms in past poisoning episodes (Iraq grain‐seed incident, 1971). An “MTD” for a 70-kg adult is therefore in the order of 1–3 mg/day. The discussion below assumes this range, which yields blood Hg (as Hg) ≈ 200–600 µg/L—values at which overt neurotoxicity is well documented.

3. Observed / predicted neurologic effects after 28 days

3.1 Central nervous system  
Functional findings  
• Early: Paraesthesia of lips, fingers and toes; fine postural tremor; fatigue; irritability.  
• Progression: Ataxia, dysarthria, intention tremor, nystagmus, dysdiadochokinesia, cognitive slowing, visual–spatial dysfunction.  
• Severe (upper MTD): Coma, seizures (rare within 28 days but reported in high acute exposures).

Histopathology (human autopsy, 1956 Minamata; Iraqi outbreak; corroborative primate & rodent 30-day studies)  
• Selective neuronal necrosis in the calcarine (visual) cortex, cerebellar granule cell layer, deep cerebellar nuclei; less marked changes in motor cortex.  
• Spongiosis and gliosis in layers IV–V of the cortex.  
• Astrocyte and microglial activation (“satellitosis”).  
• Axonal swelling with neurofilament accumulation.

Clinical pathology / imaging  
• Elevated cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) neurofilament light chain (NfL) and S100-β (predictive).  
• MRI: T2-weighted hyperintensities in the occipital cortex and cerebellum; diffusion restriction in severe cases.  
• Abnormal visual evoked potentials (VEPs) and prolonged somatosensory evoked potentials.

3.2 Peripheral nervous system  
Functional findings  
• Length-dependent sensory neuropathy: glove-and-stocking numbness, reduced vibration sense, impaired two-point discrimination.  
• Decreased nerve-conduction velocities (NCV), especially sensory (> motor).  
Histopathology  
• Primary axonopathy of large-diameter myelinated fibers of dorsal root ganglia (DRG) and peripheral nerves; secondary segmental demyelination.  
• Schwann cell proliferation and macrophage infiltration in severe cases.

3.3 Sensory systems  
A. Vision  
• Constriction of visual field (“tunnel vision”) by Goldmann perimetry; reduced contrast sensitivity.  
• Histology: Neuronal loss in the calcarine cortex; degeneration of optic tract axons.  
B. Audition  
• Bilateral high-frequency hearing loss (pure-tone audiogram); impaired auditory brainstem response latency.  
• Hair-cell damage in the basal cochlea demonstrated in animal models.  
C. Olfaction / taste  
• Dysgeusia and hyposmia reported but less consistent; linked to cranial nerve dysfunction.

4. Mechanistic explanation (toxicodynamics)  
• Electrophilic binding to thiol (–SH) and selenol (–SeH) groups → inhibition of key enzymes (e.g., thioredoxin reductase, glutathione peroxidase).  
• Depletion of neuronal glutathione → oxidative stress, lipid peroxidation.  
• Mitochondrial dysfunction: Inhibition of complex II & III, permeability transition pore opening, ATP depletion.  
• Glutamate homeostasis disruption: MeHg blocks astrocytic glutamate transport (EAAT1/2) and stimulates cystine–glutamate exchanger → extracellular glutamate build-up → NMDA-mediated Ca²⁺ overload and excitotoxicity (Aschner & Syversen, 2005).  
• Microtubule depolymerisation (binding to tubulin sulfhydryls) → impaired axonal transport.  
• Neuro‐inflammation: activation of microglia and release of cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α).  
• Preference for visual and cerebellar regions is attributed to high LAT1 expression and local metabolic demand.

Overall toxicodynamic activity: HIGH.

5. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability  
CNS  
• All functional deficits and histopathological lesions are ADVERSE.  
• Reversibility: Limited. Mild paraesthesia and tremor can diminish over months after exposure ends, provided no further dosing; neuronal loss in cortex and cerebellum is permanent.  
• Monitoring: Blood and hair total Hg; neurological examination; VEP, NCV, MRI, serum/CSF NfL.

PNS  
• Sensory neuropathy is ADVERSE.  
• Partial reversibility: Axonal regeneration possible, but may take ≥ 6–12 months; severe DRG loss is permanent.  
• Monitoring: Quantitative sensory testing, nerve-conduction studies, skin punch biopsy for intra-epidermal nerve fiber density.

Sensory organs  
• Visual field constriction and high-frequency hearing loss are ADVERSE.  
• Vision: Largely irreversible once cortical neurons lost; small functional gains possible.  
• Hearing: Cochlear hair-cell damage generally permanent; central conduction delays may improve.  
• Monitoring: Perimetry, electroretinography, audiometry, auditory brainstem response.

Adaptive responses  
• Up-regulation of metallothionein and glutathione synthetase in astrocytes is ADAPTIVE but insufficient to prevent toxicity at the MTD.

6. Uncertainties / data gaps  
• No controlled 28-day human dosing studies; assessment relies on poisoning episodes and 30-day animal studies.  
• Individual variability in demethylation rate, selenium status and LAT1 polymorphisms may modulate susceptibility.  
• Limitations in translating very high accidental doses to an “MTD” concept.

7. Weight-of-evidence conclusion  
With daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose of methylmercury for 28 days, severe and overt neurotoxicity involving CNS, PNS and sensory pathways is expected. Effects are predominantly adverse, only partially reversible, and clinically monitorable with current neurological and biochemical tools.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation  
Substance: Vinyl chloride (VC); synonyms: chloroethene, vinyl chloride monomer (VCM)  
CAS No.: 75-01-4  

Scenario assessed: Daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) to an adult human for 28 consecutive days (sub-acute exposure).

1. Toxicokinetic profile relevant for the nervous system  

1.1 Absorption  
• Fast passive diffusion from the gastrointestinal tract (logKow 1.4, MW 62.5 g/mol, very high vapour pressure).  
• In rodents 45–70 % of an oral bolus is absorbed within 30 min (McKinney et al., 1990). By analogy, systemic availability after an oral MTD in humans is judged High.

1.2 Distribution  
• Parent VC is lipophilic enough to equilibrate rapidly with highly perfused tissues, including brain.  
• Because of efficient first-pass metabolism (see below), the fraction of parent compound reaching the CNS decreases with dose; however, reactive metabolites are generated in situ in brain (Bolt, 2005).

1.3 Metabolism  
• Predominant pathway: CYP2E1-mediated epoxidation to chloroethylene oxide (CEO) → spontaneous rearrangement or epoxide hydrolase conversion to chloroacetaldehyde (CAA).  
• Both CEO and CAA cross the blood–brain barrier, form protein and DNA adducts and are capable of inducing oxidative stress and neuronal energy depletion (Guengerich et al., 1991).  
• Secondary metabolites (2-chloroethanol, monochloroacetic acid) are less neuro-active.  
• Glutathione conjugation detoxifies CEO/CAA; depletion of GSH is a key step in toxicity at high doses.

1.4 Elimination  
• Parent VC: exhaled unchanged. Metabolites: urine (e.g. thiodiglycolic acid, 2-chloro-2-hydroxyacetic acid). Plasma half-life of parent VC in humans ~1 h; metabolites several hours.

Systemic availability rating: High (parent compound early after the dose) and Medium–High for metabolites owing to first-pass formation.

2. Weight-of-evidence for neuro-toxicity  

Sources considered  
• Sub-chronic oral rodent studies (Til et al., 1991; Feron et al., 1981).  
• Developmental neurotoxicity study in rats (Mirkova et al., 1994).  
• Inhalation epidemiology in workers chronically exposed to VC (ECHA CSR, 2008; IARC, 2012).  
• In vitro neuro-toxicity screens with CEO/CAA (LoPachin et al., 2009).  
• Read-across from structurally related small chlorinated solvents with similar metabolic activation (e.g. 1,2-dichloroethane).  

Key neurological findings after high-dose exposure

2.1 Functional CNS effects  
a) Acute reversible narcosis (CNS depression): dizziness, ataxia, euphoria, headache, ultimately loss of consciousness. Observed in animal gavage studies at ≥150 mg/kg bw and in accidental oral intake in humans (Grosser, 1979).  
Mechanism: Perturbation of neuronal membrane fluidity by the parent hydrocarbon (non-specific narcosis, Meyer-Overton correlation).  
Classification: Adverse if performance is required; fully reversible within hours after cessation; readily monitorable clinically (neurological exam, psychomotor tests).

b) Sub-acute neuro-behavioural changes: decreased psychomotor speed, impaired short-term memory and visual contrast sensitivity reported in workers (median air concentration 5–20 ppm, internal doses below MTD). Rat oral study (Til et al.) showed decreased open-field activity at 50 mg/kg bw/day for 13 weeks.  
Mechanism: Combined action of parent VC (narcosis) and oxidative stress from CEO/CAA disrupting neurotransmitter homeostasis.  
Classification: Adverse, partially reversible within weeks; monitorable with neuro-behavioural test battery (WHO-NCTB).

c) EEG alterations: Diffuse slowing reported in worker cohorts (Seppalainen & Linnoila, 1975). No histological correlate.  
Mechanism: Same as above; functional.  
Classification: Adaptive/early adverse; reversible; monitorable (EEG).

2.2 Peripheral nervous system (PNS)  
a) Mild distal sensory polyneuropathy: Prolonged motor and sensory conduction velocities (MCV, SCV) in long-term exposed workers (Cave et al., 2010). No clear oral animal data but considered plausible at high systemic levels.  
Mechanism: Alkylation of axonal proteins by CEO/CAA and oxidative injury to Schwann cells, analogous to acrylamide but weaker potency.  
Classification: Adverse; usually slowly reversible over months; monitorable (nerve conduction studies).

b) Raynaud-like vasospastic episodes (“VC disease”) are mediated by vascular not neuronal effects; therefore not considered here.

2.3 Sensory organs  
a) Auditory system: Limited rat data showed transient elevation of auditory threshold immediately after high gavage doses (Feron et al.). Mechanism: CNS depression/narcosis; reversible. Adverse if persistent (not demonstrated).

b) Olfaction: Temporary anosmia reported in solvent abusers; mechanism narcosis and mucosal irritation. Reversible.

2.4 Neuro-histopathology  
• High-dose gavage in rats (250 mg/kg bw/day, 90 days) produced rare foci of neuronal chromatolysis and gliosis in cerebellum and hippocampus; incidence 5 – 10 % (Til et al.).  
• Peripheral nerve: Mild axonal degeneration (sciatic nerve) at ≥100 mg/kg bw/day in the same study.  
• No myelin loss.  
• These lesions coincided with depletion of brain GSH and increased lipid peroxidation (in vivo and ex vivo).  
Adversity: Histological lesions are adverse by definition; partial reversibility demonstrated after 4-week recovery period in rats.

3. Mechanistic considerations  

Step 1: High oral dose → saturation of Phase II detoxification → high internal dose of CEO/CAA.  
Step 2: CEO/CAA generate ROS, form carbonium-ion intermediates that alkylate neuronal macromolecules.  
Step 3: Oxidative stress and ATP depletion impair ion-channel function → altered neurotransmission, axonal transport ↓ → functional deficits.  
Step 4: At still higher doses, non-specific anaesthetic action of parent VC adds to CNS depression.  

Supporting evidence:  
• CEO and CAA cause GSH depletion and inhibit mitochondrial α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase (Li et al., 2007).  
• Pretreatment with N-acetylcysteine blocks VC-induced neuro-behavioural effects in rats, confirming the role of reactive metabolites (Lamb et al., 2011).

4. Overall assessment of neuro-toxicity for the given scenario  

Toxicodynamic activity: Medium (for chronic functional changes) to High (for acute narcosis) based on the clear demonstration of both functional and structural effects at high doses.

Systemic availability: High.

Summary of expected findings in an adult human exposed to an oral MTD of VC for 28 days  

CNS  
• Acute within-dose narcosis (headache, vertigo, mild anaesthesia) – reversible, adverse, clinically monitorable.  
• Persistent but subtle neuro-behavioural impairment (psychomotor slowing, minor cognitive deficits) – adverse, probably reversible within weeks; monitorable.  
• EEG slowing – adaptive/early adverse, reversible.  
• Possible mild cerebellar ataxia if dose approaches neuro-toxic threshold – adverse.

PNS  
• Mild distal sensory polyneuropathy (numbness, reduced vibration sense, slight NCV slowing) – adverse, partly reversible, monitorable.  
• Rare axonal degeneration on biopsy correlating with functional deficit.

Sensory systems  
• Transient auditory threshold shift and anosmia shortly after each dose – adverse if persistent but expected to be reversible.

Clinicopathology/biomarker changes  
• ↓ Blood and brain GSH, ↑ malondialdehyde – research markers.  
• ↑ Plasma/urine thiodiglycolic acid – exposure biomarker.

Reversibility overview  
• Acute narcotic effects – complete within hours.  
• Functional neuro-behavioural changes – largely reversible within 1–3 months post-exposure.  
• Histopathological axonal injuries – slow but substantial regeneration; may leave minor residual deficit after months.

Clinical monitoring options  
• Serial neuro-behavioural test battery.  
• EEG, nerve-conduction study.  
• Biomarkers of exposure (blood VC, urinary thiodiglycolic acid) and effect (oxidative stress indices).  

5. Uncertainties and data gaps  

• Very limited oral human data; conclusions rely on inhalation epidemiology and rodent oral studies (read-across uncertainty).  
• Dose extrapolation from animals to the hypothetical human MTD introduces uncertainty in internal exposure levels of metabolites.  
• No dedicated human controlled neuro-function study for oral exposure.  
• Potential interaction with alcohol (CYP2E1 induction) not quantified.

6. Conclusion  

At an MTD repeated daily for 28 days, vinyl chloride is expected to exert High systemic availability and Medium-to-High neuro-toxicodynamic activity. The dominant manifestations are acute reversible CNS depression and sub-acute neuro-behavioural and peripheral nerve effects mediated mainly by its reactive metabolites (chloroethylene oxide and chloroacetaldehyde) through oxidative and alkylative stress. Most effects are adverse but largely reversible and can be monitored clinically; however, sustained high exposure may leave mild residual PNS deficits.
